    • Visibility: you can't optimize what you can't see. Tag everything. Use cost allocation tags to attribute spend to teams, services, and environments. Implement showback (show teams their cost) or chargeback (charge teams their cost).
    • Right-sizing: the single biggest cost optimization lever. Most organizations run instances 2-4x larger than needed. Use cloud provider recommendations (Compute Optimizer, Azure Advisor) and monitoring data to right-size.
    • Scheduling: development and staging environments don't need to run 24/7. Schedule them to shut down outside business hours. This alone saves 65% on non-production compute costs (nights + weekends).
    • Pricing models: use the right pricing model for each workload. On-demand for variable/testing workloads. Reserved/Savings Plans for steady-state (30-72% savings). Spot/preemptible for fault-tolerant batch (60-90% savings).
    • Storage tiering: move data to the cheapest appropriate tier. S3 Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier for archives, delete unneeded data. Storage is cheap per GB but expensive at petabyte scale.
    • Architecture optimization: serverless for sporadic workloads (pay only for execution). ARM-based instances (Graviton on AWS, Ampere on Azure/GCP) for 20-40% better price/performance. Containerization for higher instance utilization (run multiple services per instance instead of one service per instance).
    • Data transfer: minimize cross-region and cross-AZ data transfer. Use VPC endpoints to avoid NAT Gateway costs. Place compute close to data. Data transfer costs are the most commonly overlooked expense.
    • Eliminate waste: unused EBS volumes, unattached Elastic IPs, idle load balancers, old snapshots, unused Reserved Instances. Use tools like AWS Trusted Advisor, Cost Anomaly Detection, or third-party tools (Infracost, Spot.io) to identify waste.

FinOps Practice

  • Interview-specific:
    • FinOps is a cultural practice, not just tooling. It requires collaboration between engineering (optimize architecture), finance (forecast and budget), and leadership (prioritize cost efficiency).
    • Three phases: Inform (allocate costs, create visibility), Optimize (right-size, reserve, eliminate waste), Operate (continuous governance, budgets, alerts).
    • Unit economics: track cost per transaction, cost per customer, cost per API call - not just total spend. Total spend may increase as the business grows, but cost-per-unit should decrease over time.
    • Anomaly detection: automatically detect unexpected cost spikes. A developer accidentally launches 100 large instances, a misconfigured auto-scaler, a DDoS attack generating massive data transfer charges. Catch within hours, not at the end of the month.
  1. Multi-Cloud and Cloud-Native Patterns

Multi-Cloud Strategy

  • Interview-specific:
    • Why multi-cloud: avoid vendor lock-in, leverage best-of-breed services (BigQuery for analytics, AWS for general compute, Azure for Microsoft ecosystem integration), regulatory requirements (certain data must stay with a specific provider or region), negotiating leverage with cloud providers.
    • Why NOT multi-cloud: operational complexity is enormous. Each cloud has different networking, IAM, storage, and service models. Teams must maintain expertise across all providers. Lowest common denominator architecture loses cloud-specific advantages. Most organizations that claim multi-cloud are actually "multi-cloud by accident" (acquired companies on different clouds) rather than "multi-cloud by design."
    • The honest interview answer: "True multi-cloud (same workload running on multiple clouds simultaneously) is rarely justified. More common and practical is 'cloud-appropriate' - primary workloads on one cloud, specific workloads on another where it has a clear advantage, and portability at the container/Kubernetes layer for future flexibility."
    • Portable layers: containers and Kubernetes are the primary portability mechanism. Application code in containers is cloud-agnostic. Kubernetes runs on all clouds. But the moment you use AWS Lambda, DynamoDB, or SQS, you're locked in. The trade-off: cloud-specific managed services are easier to operate and more feature-rich, but reduce portability. Portable alternatives (Kafka instead of SQS, PostgreSQL instead of DynamoDB) are more work to operate but more portable.

Abstraction Layers

  • Interview-specific:
    • Infrastructure abstraction: Terraform supports multiple clouds. Define infrastructure in a cloud-agnostic way - mostly. In practice, each provider's resources have unique configurations, and true portability requires significant abstraction effort.
    • Application abstraction: use standard APIs (S3 API is a de facto standard - many storage systems implement S3-compatible APIs). Use message queue abstractions (cloud-agnostic message interfaces over SQS, Azure Service Bus, or GCP Pub/Sub).
    • Platform abstraction: Kubernetes provides a consistent deployment platform across clouds. Knative provides serverless abstractions on Kubernetes. Crossplane extends Kubernetes to manage cloud resources declaratively.
    • The pragmatic approach: abstract where the cost of switching is high and the likelihood of switching is realistic. Don't abstract where the cost of abstraction exceeds the cost of migration.
  1. Serverless Architecture Patterns (Deep Dive)

Event-Driven Serverless Patterns

  • Interview-specific:
    • API Gateway + Lambda: the most common serverless pattern. API Gateway handles HTTP requests, authentication, rate limiting, and routes to Lambda functions. Lambda executes business logic. DynamoDB stores data. This can handle significant scale with zero operational overhead.
    • S3 Event + Lambda: file upload triggers processing. A user uploads an image to S3, Lambda is triggered, resizes the image, and stores the result back in S3. Completely automated, scales to any number of concurrent uploads.
    • SQS + Lambda: decouple producers from consumers. An API writes messages to SQS. Lambda polls SQS and processes messages. SQS handles retries (messages return to the queue if Lambda fails). Dead letter queue catches messages that fail repeatedly.
    • Step Functions (AWS) / Durable Functions (Azure): orchestrate multi-step workflows with error handling, branching, parallel execution, and human approval steps. "On new order: charge payment → reserve inventory → schedule shipping → send confirmation. If payment fails: notify customer." Visual workflow definition. Built-in retry and error handling. State machine semantics.
    • Event Bridge: serverless event bus. Route events from AWS services, SaaS partners, and custom applications to Lambda, SQS, Step Functions, or other targets. Event filtering, transformation, and fan-out.

Serverless Anti-Patterns

  • Interview-specific:
    • Lambda monolith: a single Lambda function handling all API routes with a framework router inside it. Loses the benefits of independent scaling and deployment per function. But pragmatic for small teams - reduces cold start surface area.
    • Synchronous chain: Lambda A calls Lambda B calls Lambda C synchronously. Latency accumulates. If any function fails, the entire chain fails. Better: use event-driven (asynchronous) composition where Lambda A publishes an event, Lambda B consumes it independently.
    • Over-orchestration: Step Functions for simple linear workflows where a single Lambda would suffice. Don't add orchestration overhead for trivial sequences.
    • Ignoring cold starts: designing latency-sensitive user-facing APIs on Lambda without provisioned concurrency or warm-up strategies. Users experience intermittent slow responses.
  1. Cloud Governance and Landing Zones

Cloud Landing Zone

  • Interview-specific:
    • A landing zone is the pre-configured, secure, multi-account cloud environment that serves as the foundation for all workloads. It establishes the baseline for security, networking, identity, logging, and cost management before any application is deployed.
    • AWS Control Tower / Azure Landing Zones / GCP Cloud Foundation Toolkit: automated frameworks for setting up landing zones with best-practice configurations.
    • Multi-account strategy: separate accounts for different purposes - security (centralized logging, audit), networking (shared VPCs, transit gateway, DNS), shared services (CI/CD, artifact registries), and workload accounts per team or environment. Account-level isolation provides the strongest blast radius reduction - a compromised workload account can't affect the security account.
    • Account vending: automated process for creating new accounts with standard configuration (IAM baseline, networking, logging, budget alerts, compliance rules). A new team requests an account → automated pipeline provisions it with all standards → team can deploy within hours, not weeks.
    • Guardrails: preventive (SCPs that block prohibited actions) and detective (Config rules that detect non-compliance). Both should be in place from day one. It's much harder to retrofit guardrails after teams have been operating without them.

Organizational Policies

  • Interview-specific:
    • Service Control Policies (AWS): organization-wide permission boundaries. "No one in any account can disable CloudTrail." "No one can create resources in regions outside of our approved list." "No one can create unencrypted S3 buckets."
    • Tag policies: enforce consistent tagging across the organization. Required tags (Environment, Team, CostCenter) with allowed values (Environment must be one of dev, staging, production).
    • Backup policies: ensure all critical resources are backed up according to organizational standards. AWS Backup with organization-wide policies.
    • Central logging: all accounts ship CloudTrail logs (API activity), VPC Flow Logs (network traffic), and application logs to a centralized security account. This account has strict access controls - even account administrators in workload accounts cannot delete or modify their audit logs.
  1. Edge Computing and CDN Architecture

Edge Computing

  • Interview-specific:
    • Edge computing processes data closer to where it's generated rather than sending everything to a centralized cloud. Use when: latency requirements are extremely tight (sub-10ms), bandwidth costs of sending all data to cloud are prohibitive (video streams, IoT sensor data), or offline capability is needed (factory floor, remote locations).
    • AWS Wavelength (compute at 5G cell towers), AWS Outposts (AWS infrastructure on-premises), Azure Stack Edge, Google Distributed Cloud: cloud-managed infrastructure at the edge. Run the same APIs and services as in the cloud but at edge locations.
    • Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda@Edge, CloudFront Functions: run code at CDN edge locations. Use for: A/B testing (route users to different versions without origin round-trip), authentication (validate tokens at the edge), personalization (customize responses based on geolocation), and URL rewriting.
    • The edge-cloud spectrum: not all computation belongs at the edge or in the cloud. Raw data processing and time-sensitive decisions happen at the edge. Aggregation, ML model training, and long-term storage happen in the cloud. The architecture defines what runs where and how data flows between tiers.

Summary - Interview Signal Map

Topic

Junior Signal

Senior/Architect Signal

Service models

"Use EC2 for everything"

Matches service model to workload requirements with cost-benefit reasoning

Architecture

"Lift and shift to cloud"

Cloud-native design with twelve-factor principles, immutable infrastructure, cattle-not-pets

IaC

"Click in the console"

Terraform modules, remote state, GitOps, drift detection, environment management

Kubernetes

"Deploy to K8s"

Resource management, HPA/VPA, network policies, stateful workloads, right-sizing

Security

"Use IAM"

Least privilege, cross-account roles, service mesh mTLS, secrets rotation, CSPM, SCPs

Networking

"Put it in a VPC"

Multi-AZ/multi-region design, hybrid connectivity, service mesh, zero-trust

Cost

Not mentioned

FinOps practice, right-sizing, reserved capacity, spot instances, unit economics, tagging

Migration

"Move to cloud"

7 R's assessment, migration sequencing, database migration patterns, strangler fig

DR

"We have backups"

RPO/RTO-driven architecture, chaos engineering, multi-region failover, tested recovery

Serverless

"Use Lambda"

Event-driven patterns, cold start mitigation, Step Functions orchestration, anti-patterns

Multi-cloud

"Use all clouds"

Pragmatic assessment of portability vs managed service benefits, abstraction cost analysis

Governance

Not mentioned

Landing zones, multi-account strategy, SCPs, automated guardrails, account vending

 

 

 

Microservices Excellence

  1. Microservices Fundamentals and When to Use Them

What Interviewers Are Testing

They don't want buzzword fluency. They want to see that you understand WHY microservices exist, WHEN they're appropriate, WHEN they're harmful, and that you've experienced the real operational cost - not just read about the theoretical benefits.

Defining Microservices Correctly

What microservices actually are: A system decomposed into small, independently deployable services, each owning its own data, organized around business capabilities, communicating over the network. The defining characteristic is INDEPENDENT DEPLOYABILITY - if you can't deploy Service A without coordinating with Service B, you don't have microservices. You have a distributed monolith.

What microservices are NOT:

  • Small services (size is irrelevant - what matters is boundary alignment)
  • Services that share a database (this is a distributed monolith with extra network hops)
  • REST APIs (communication mechanism, not architecture)
  • Docker containers (packaging format, not architecture)
  • The automatic solution to scaling problems

The microservices trade-off equation: You're trading the complexity of a large codebase (monolith) for the complexity of a distributed system. You lose: simple function calls, ACID transactions, straightforward debugging, easy refactoring across boundaries. You gain: independent deployability, independent scalability, technology heterogeneity, autonomous team ownership, fault isolation.

  • Interview-specific: The single most impressive thing you can say in a microservices interview is "I've seen microservices fail." Then explain why - premature decomposition, team not ready for operational complexity, distributed monolith anti-pattern. This signals real-world experience, not textbook knowledge.

When Microservices Are Right

  • Interview-specific: Microservices solve organizational problems as much as technical ones. They're justified when:
    • Large engineering organization (30+ engineers): Multiple teams need to work independently without stepping on each other. Microservices align with Conway's Law - each team owns one or more services end-to-end.
    • Well-understood domain: Bounded contexts are clearly identified and stable. If you're still discovering the domain, you'll draw boundaries wrong - and refactoring across network boundaries is 10x harder than refactoring within a monolith.
    • Different scaling requirements: The search service needs 50 instances, the admin service needs 2. In a monolith, you scale everything together. With microservices, you scale each independently.
    • Different technology requirements: The ML recommendation engine benefits from Python. The payment processing system needs a strongly-typed language. Microservices allow polyglot technology choices per service.
    • Independent release cadence: Team A releases 10 times/day. Team B releases weekly. In a monolith with coupled deployment, the slowest team dictates everyone's pace. Microservices decouple release cycles.
    • Operational maturity exists: The organization has CI/CD pipelines, container orchestration, centralized logging, distributed tracing, monitoring, and incident response processes. Without this foundation, microservices are operational suicide.

When Microservices Are Wrong

  • Interview-specific: Being able to articulate when NOT to use microservices is more impressive than advocating for them.
    • Small team (under 10-15 engineers): The operational overhead of microservices exceeds the organizational benefit. A team of 6 engineers managing 15 microservices spends more time on infrastructure than features.
    • Greenfield project with unclear domain: You don't know where the boundaries should be. Premature decomposition creates wrong boundaries that are painful to fix across network boundaries. Start with a modular monolith, extract services as boundaries become clear.
    • No operational infrastructure: If you don't have automated CI/CD, container orchestration, centralized logging, and distributed tracing - build those first. Microservices without operational maturity means you can't deploy reliably, can't debug production issues, and can't monitor system health.
    • Tight coupling between components: If every user request requires synchronous calls across 8 services, you haven't decomposed well. You've created a distributed monolith that's slower and harder to debug than the monolith you started with.
    • Simple domain: A CRUD application with straightforward business logic doesn't benefit from microservices. The architectural complexity far exceeds the domain complexity.

The Microservices Spectrum

  • Interview-specific: It's not binary - monolith OR microservices. There's a spectrum:
    • Monolith: Single deployable unit. All logic together. Simple. Right for small teams and new projects.
    • Modular Monolith: Single deployable unit with strict internal module boundaries. Each module has a public API, owns its data, hides internals. Enforced by language-level module systems and architectural tests. You get 80% of the organizational benefit of microservices at 20% of the operational cost.
    • Service-Based Architecture: A few large services (5-12) organized around major business domains. Coarser-grained than microservices. Each service may be a modular monolith internally. Fewer services = less operational overhead while still enabling team autonomy for major domains.
    • Microservices: Many small services (dozens to hundreds) with narrow responsibilities. Maximum autonomy and scalability. Maximum operational complexity.
    • Nano-services (anti-pattern): Services so small they have no meaningful logic. Every function is a service. Network overhead dominates. Impossible to reason about. Don't do this.

The mature recommendation: start at the left of the spectrum and move right only as justified by organizational growth, domain understanding, and operational maturity.

Conway's Law and Team Topology

Conway's Law: "Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations." This isn't a suggestion - it's an observed law about organizational gravity. A system's architecture will inevitably mirror the team structure that builds it.

Reverse Conway Maneuver: Deliberately structure teams to produce the desired architecture. If you want independently deployable services, create autonomous cross-functional teams (frontend, backend, database, QA, ops - all within the same team) aligned to business capabilities.

Team Topologies (Matthew Skelton & Manuel Pais):

  • Stream-aligned team: The primary team type. Aligned to a business domain or value stream (Order team, Payment team, Search team). Owns one or more services end-to-end. Responsible for building, deploying, and operating their services.
  • Platform team: Provides internal tools and infrastructure that stream-aligned teams consume. CI/CD pipelines, Kubernetes platform, observability stack, service mesh. Their goal: reduce cognitive load on stream-aligned teams so they can focus on business logic.
  • Enabling team: Helps stream-aligned teams adopt new capabilities. Coaches on new technologies, practices, or patterns. Temporary engagement - they build capability in the stream-aligned team, then move on.
  • Complicated subsystem team: Owns a technically complex component that requires specialist expertise (ML model, video encoding engine, cryptographic system). Used sparingly - only when the complexity justifies dedicated specialists.
  • Interview-specific: "How should teams be organized for microservices?" Answer with Team Topologies. Explain that stream-aligned teams own services, platform teams reduce operational burden, and the number of services per team should be manageable (2-5 services per team, depending on complexity). If a team owns 15 services, they're spread too thin and quality suffers.
  1. Service Decomposition Strategies

How to Draw Boundaries (The Hardest Problem)

  • Interview-specific: Service decomposition is the single most consequential decision in microservices architecture. Bad boundaries create a distributed monolith - all the complexity of microservices with none of the benefits. Good boundaries enable independent development, deployment, and scaling.

Decomposition by Business Capability

Align services to what the business does, not how technology is structured. Business capabilities are stable over time even as technology changes.

Example for e-commerce:

  • Order Management (placing, tracking, modifying orders)
  • Payment Processing (charging, refunding, payment method management)
  • Inventory Management (stock tracking, reservation, replenishment)
  • Catalog Management (product information, categories, pricing)
  • Customer Management (profiles, preferences, address book)
  • Shipping and Fulfillment (carrier selection, tracking, delivery)
  • Notification Service (email, SMS, push notifications)
  • Search Service (product search, filtering, ranking)
  • Interview-specific: Notice these align with departments or business functions, not technology layers. There's no "Database Service" or "API Service." Each capability has its own data, its own logic, and its own reason to change independently.

Decomposition by Subdomain (Domain-Driven Design)

Use DDD's strategic design to identify bounded contexts. Each bounded context becomes a service (or a small group of services).

Core Domain: What makes the business unique. Invest the best engineers. Custom-build with the best patterns. For Uber: the matching and pricing algorithm. For Netflix: the recommendation engine.

Supporting Domain: Necessary but not differentiating. Build in-house but with less investment. For an e-commerce platform: inventory management, order processing.

Generic Domain: Solved problems. Use off-the-shelf solutions. Authentication (Keycloak, Auth0), email (SendGrid), payments (Stripe).

  • Interview-specific: Subdomain classification directly impacts architectural investment. Don't over-engineer generic domains (nobody needs a custom email system). Don't under-invest in core domains (your competitive advantage deserves the best engineering). This alignment of effort to business value is what separates architects from developers.

Decomposition by Volatility

Isolate components that change frequently from those that are stable.

  • Interview-specific: If the pricing rules change weekly but the product catalog structure hasn't changed in two years, pricing should be a separate service. Changes to pricing don't require redeploying the catalog. This reduces deployment risk and enables faster iteration where the business needs it most.

Decomposition by Data Ownership

Each service owns the data it needs. If two proposed services need to write to the same table, they're probably one service - or the data model needs refactoring.

  • Interview-specific: Data ownership is the hardest part of decomposition. The questions to ask: "Which service is the source of truth for this data? Which service writes this data? Which services only read it?" The writer is the owner. Readers access data through the owner's API or consume events from the owner.

Decomposition Anti-Patterns

Entity-based services: One service per database entity (UserService, AddressService, PhoneNumberService). Creates excessive inter-service communication for any meaningful operation. A "create user with address and phone" requires orchestrating three services. This is decomposition by data model, not by business capability.

Technology-layer services: API Gateway Service, Business Logic Service, Database Service. This is a distributed three-tier architecture - not microservices. Each "service" must change for every feature. Independent deployability is impossible.

Too-small services (nano-services): Every function is a service. Enormous network overhead, impossible to debug, deploy, or reason about. If a service has only one endpoint and 50 lines of code, it's probably too small.

Distributed monolith: Services that must be deployed together, share a database, or fail together. You've taken on all the complexity of a distributed system with none of the benefits. Symptoms: coordinated deployments, shared database schemas, every change requires updating multiple services, one service failure cascades to all others.

  • Interview-specific: "What's the biggest mistake you've seen in microservices decomposition?" Having a prepared answer with a specific anti-pattern and how you resolved it is extremely valuable. Example: "We had entity-based services where a single user action required 7 synchronous service calls. We consolidated into 3 capability-based services, reducing latency by 80% and dramatically simplifying the deployment process."

Heuristics for Right-Sizing Services

  • A service should be ownable by one team (not shared across teams)
  • A service should be deployable independently (no coordinated releases)
  • A service should have a clear, cohesive business responsibility expressible in one sentence
  • A service should be replaceable in 2-4 weeks if rewritten from scratch (not a hard rule, but a useful gut check)
  • A service should change for one business reason, not multiple unrelated reasons
  • If two services always change together, they should probably be one service
  • If a service can't function without synchronously calling another specific service, the boundary may be wrong
  1. Inter-Service Communication

Synchronous Communication

REST (HTTP/JSON):

The most common communication style. Resource-oriented. Widely understood. Excellent tooling.

  • Interview-specific:
    • REST is simple and familiar but creates temporal coupling - both the caller and the callee must be available simultaneously. If the callee is down, the caller fails (unless you have fallback logic).
    • REST over HTTP/1.1 has overhead: text-based headers, JSON serialization/deserialization, no multiplexing (one request per connection or connection pooling).
    • Use REST for: public-facing APIs, BFF layers, simple request-response interactions, integrations with third parties.
    • Richardson Maturity Model: Level 0 (single URI, POST everything - basically RPC), Level 1 (resources - /orders, /users), Level 2 (proper HTTP verbs and status codes - GET for reads, POST for creation, 201 for created, 404 for not found), Level 3 (HATEOAS - responses include links to related actions). Most production APIs are Level 2.
    • API design best practices: use nouns for resources (/orders, not /getOrders), use plural nouns (/orders/{id}, not /order/{id}), use HTTP status codes correctly (don't return 200 with an error body), version your API (URI path /v1/orders or Accept header), use pagination for collections, support filtering and sorting via query parameters, return consistent error format across all services.

gRPC (Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2):

Strongly typed contracts defined in .proto files. Binary serialization (much smaller and faster than JSON). HTTP/2 multiplexing (multiple requests on a single connection). Code generation for clients and servers. Supports streaming (client, server, and bidirectional).

  • Interview-specific:
    • gRPC is significantly faster than REST/JSON for internal service-to-service communication. Binary serialization is 5-10x smaller and 5-10x faster to serialize/deserialize than JSON. HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminates connection overhead.
    • Strongly typed contracts prevent integration errors at compile time rather than runtime. If a service changes its API, the consumer's build breaks immediately.
    • Streaming is a unique capability: server-streaming (service pushes multiple responses), client-streaming (client sends multiple requests), bidirectional (both sides stream simultaneously). Use for: real-time data feeds, file upload in chunks, long-running operations with progress updates.
    • Drawbacks: not browser-friendly (gRPC-Web exists but adds complexity), harder to debug (binary protocol - can't inspect with curl), less ecosystem support for tooling (API gateways, documentation), steeper learning curve.
    • Use gRPC for: internal microservice-to-microservice communication, high-performance paths, streaming requirements, polyglot environments (code generation for many languages from one .proto file).

GraphQL:

Client specifies exactly what data it needs. Single endpoint. No over-fetching or under-fetching.

  • Interview-specific:
    • GraphQL is not a microservices communication protocol - it's a client-facing API layer. Typically used in a BFF pattern: the GraphQL server sits between frontend clients and backend microservices. The frontend sends one GraphQL query, the GraphQL server decomposes it into multiple microservice calls and assembles the response.
    • Apollo Federation: compose a single GraphQL schema from multiple microservices. Each service defines its part of the schema. The Apollo Gateway merges them and routes query fields to the appropriate service. This enables each team to own their portion of the GraphQL schema independently.
    • N+1 problem: a GraphQL query for "orders with products" might fetch 100 orders, then make 100 separate calls to the product service (one per order). DataLoader solves this by batching - collect all product IDs, make one batch call to the product service.
    • When to use GraphQL: complex frontend data requirements, multiple client types (web, mobile, third-party) with different data needs, deep data hierarchies where REST would require many endpoints or over-fetch.
    • When NOT to use: simple CRUD APIs, internal service-to-service communication (use gRPC or events instead), file upload/download, real-time streaming.

Asynchronous Communication

Message Queues (Point-to-Point):

A producer sends a message to a queue. Exactly one consumer receives and processes each message. The message is deleted after processing.

RabbitMQ, Amazon SQS, Azure Service Bus.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Use queues for task distribution: "process this order," "send this email," "resize this image." Each task should be processed exactly once by one worker.
    • Competing consumers pattern: multiple consumer instances read from the same queue. The queue distributes messages across consumers for parallel processing. Adding consumers increases throughput linearly.
    • Dead letter queue (DLQ): messages that fail processing after a configurable number of retries are moved to a DLQ for inspection. Never silently drop failed messages - route them to a DLQ, alert on DLQ depth, and investigate.
    • Visibility timeout (SQS): when a consumer receives a message, it becomes invisible to other consumers for a timeout period. If the consumer finishes processing, it deletes the message. If the consumer crashes, the message becomes visible again and another consumer picks it up. This provides at-least-once delivery without coordination.
    • RabbitMQ vs SQS: RabbitMQ supports complex routing (exchanges, topic-based routing, headers-based routing), priority queues, and multiple protocols (AMQP). SQS is fully managed with zero operational overhead, unlimited throughput, but simpler routing (standard queues and FIFO queues).

Event Streaming (Pub/Sub with Durability):

A producer publishes an event to a topic. Multiple independent consumer groups can subscribe and process events independently. Events are retained (not deleted on consumption) and can be replayed.

Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, Azure Event Hubs, Google Pub/Sub, Apache Pulsar.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Fundamental difference from queues: in a queue, each message is consumed by one consumer and deleted. In an event stream, events are retained and consumed by multiple independent consumer groups. OrderService publishes "OrderPlaced." InventoryService, NotificationService, and AnalyticsService each consume it independently without interfering with each other.
    • Event replay: consumers can rewind to an earlier offset and reprocess events. This enables: rebuilding a service's state from scratch, populating a new service with historical data, recovering from a bug (fix the bug, replay events, recompute correct state).
    • Kafka partitioning: each topic has multiple partitions for parallelism. Events with the same key (e.g., order_id) go to the same partition, guaranteeing ordering per entity. A consumer group has one consumer per partition. Adding partitions (up to a limit) increases parallelism.
    • Retention: events are kept for a configurable period (hours, days, weeks, or forever). Unlike traditional message queues, consuming doesn't delete the event. This makes Kafka suitable as a system of record, not just a transport mechanism.

Event Types and Design

Event Notification (Thin Event):

A lightweight signal that something happened. Contains minimal data - just enough to identify what happened and to what.

{ "eventType": "OrderPlaced", "orderId": "o-12345", "timestamp": "2025-03-04T14:32:07Z" }

  • Interview-specific: Consumers that need more data must call back to the source service's API. This keeps events small and loosely coupled - the producer doesn't need to know what data consumers need. But it creates a dependency: the source service must be available when consumers process the event. If many consumers call back, the source service gets high read load.

Event-Carried State Transfer (Fat Event):

The event carries all data the consumers might need. No callbacks required.

{ "eventType": "OrderPlaced", "orderId": "o-12345", "customerId": "c-678", "items": [...], "shippingAddress": {...}, "totalAmount": 99.99, "timestamp": "..." }

  • Interview-specific: Consumers are fully self-contained - they don't need to call the source service. This maximizes decoupling and availability. But events are larger (more bandwidth, more storage), and consumers may receive data they don't need. Schema changes affect all consumers.

Domain Events:

Business-meaningful events expressed in ubiquitous language. "OrderPlaced," "PaymentReceived," "ShipmentDispatched." These are not CRUD events (OrderCreated, OrderUpdated, OrderDeleted) - they carry business meaning.

  • Interview-specific: "OrderCancelled" is a domain event. "OrderUpdated with status=CANCELLED" is a CRUD event. Domain events are more meaningful to consumers because they express what happened in business terms, not in database terms. A consumer handling "OrderCancelled" knows exactly what to do. A consumer handling "OrderUpdated" needs to inspect the payload to determine what changed and whether it cares.

Change Data Capture (CDC) Events:

Events generated from database changes (inserts, updates, deletes) captured from the database's transaction log.

  • Interview-specific: CDC events are the bridge between traditional database-centric systems and event-driven architecture. Use Debezium to capture changes from PostgreSQL/MySQL and publish to Kafka. Consumers see every database change as an event without the source application needing modification. This is how you retrofit event-driven architecture onto legacy systems.

Choosing Communication Style

  • Interview-specific: Use this decision framework:
    • Need immediate response? → Synchronous (REST/gRPC)
    • Can the caller proceed without waiting? → Asynchronous (queue/event)
    • One consumer processes each message? → Message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ)
    • Multiple independent consumers need the same event? → Event stream (Kafka)
    • Need to replay historical events? → Event stream with retention (Kafka)
    • High performance internal communication? → gRPC
    • Public API for external consumers? → REST
    • Complex frontend data requirements? → GraphQL (BFF layer)

Most real systems use MULTIPLE styles. REST for public APIs, gRPC for internal synchronous calls, Kafka for asynchronous event-driven communication. The choice is per-interaction, not system-wide.

  1. Data Management in Microservices

Database-Per-Service Pattern

The rule: Each service owns its database. No other service can access that database directly - not for reads, not for writes. If Service B needs data from Service A, it calls Service A's API or consumes Service A's events.

Why this is non-negotiable:

  • Shared databases create invisible coupling. If Service A and Service B both write to the same table, deploying a schema change requires coordinating both services.
  • Shared databases make independent deployment impossible. A migration that Service A needs might break Service B.
  • Shared databases create performance coupling. Service A's heavy queries degrade Service B's performance.
  • Without database isolation, you don't have microservices - you have a distributed monolith.
  • Interview-specific: "But what if two services need the same data?" This is the most common question. Answer: the service that is the source of truth for that data owns it. Other services either call its API for real-time access or maintain their own local copy updated via events. Yes, this means data duplication. That's a deliberate trade-off - duplication of data for decoupling of services. The alternative (shared database) defeats the entire purpose of microservices.

Polyglot Persistence in Practice

  • Interview-specific: Each service chooses the database technology that best fits its access patterns:
    • Order Service → PostgreSQL (transactional, relational queries, ACID guarantees)
    • Product Catalog → MongoDB (flexible schema, nested product attributes vary by category)
    • Session Service → Redis (sub-millisecond lookup by session key)
    • Search Service → Elasticsearch (full-text search, faceted navigation)
    • Analytics Service → ClickHouse or BigQuery (columnar, aggregation-heavy)
    • Recommendation Service → Neo4j (graph traversal of user-product relationships)
    • Event Store → Kafka or EventStoreDB (append-only, ordered events)

The operational cost: each database technology requires expertise to operate, monitor, tune, backup, and troubleshoot. Only adopt a new database technology when the access pattern genuinely warrants it, not for novelty.

Saga Pattern (Deep Dive)

The problem: In a monolith, a single database transaction ensures that "debit account A AND credit account B" either both happen or neither happens. In microservices, there's no single database. Account A is in PaymentService's database. Account B is in another service's database. You can't wrap a distributed operation in a single ACID transaction.

The solution: A saga - a sequence of local transactions coordinated through events or an orchestrator. Each step performs a local transaction and publishes an event. If any step fails, compensating transactions undo the effects of previous steps.

Choreography-based Saga:

Each service listens for events and acts independently. No central coordinator.

Example - order placement:

  1. OrderService creates order (status: PENDING) → publishes OrderCreated
  2. PaymentService hears OrderCreated → charges payment → publishes PaymentCharged
  3. InventoryService hears PaymentCharged → reserves stock → publishes StockReserved
  4. ShippingService hears StockReserved → schedules shipment → publishes ShipmentScheduled
  5. OrderService hears ShipmentScheduled → updates order (status: CONFIRMED)

If step 3 fails (out of stock):

  • InventoryService publishes StockReservationFailed
  • PaymentService hears StockReservationFailed → refunds payment → publishes PaymentRefunded
  • OrderService hears PaymentRefunded → marks order as FAILED → notifies customer
  • Interview-specific:
    • Pros: No single point of coordination. Loose coupling. Each service is autonomous.
    • Cons: Hard to understand the complete flow (it's distributed across multiple services' event handlers). Debugging is difficult - you need distributed tracing to follow the event chain. Complex failure scenarios - what if the compensating transaction fails? Adding a new step requires modifying multiple services' event subscriptions.
    • Best for: Simple sagas with 3-4 steps. Well-established domains where the flow is unlikely to change.

Orchestration-based Saga:

A central orchestrator (saga coordinator) explicitly directs each step. It tells each service what to do and handles compensation if any step fails.

Example - order placement:

  1. OrderOrchestrator receives "place order" command
  2. OrderOrchestrator tells PaymentService: "charge payment" → waits for response
  3. OrderOrchestrator tells InventoryService: "reserve stock" → waits for response
  4. OrderOrchestrator tells ShippingService: "schedule shipment" → waits for response
  5. If all succeed: OrderOrchestrator marks order as CONFIRMED
  6. If step 3 fails: OrderOrchestrator tells PaymentService: "refund payment"
  • Interview-specific:
    • Pros: The complete workflow is visible in one place (the orchestrator). Easier to understand, debug, and modify. Compensation logic is centralized.
    • Cons: The orchestrator is a single point of coordination (though it can be made resilient). Risk of the orchestrator becoming a "god service" that knows too much about other services' internals. Can create coupling if not designed carefully.
    • Best for: Complex sagas with 5+ steps, branching logic, timeout handling, and complex compensation.
    • Tools: Temporal (the leading choice - durable execution, automatic retries, visibility into saga state), Camunda, AWS Step Functions. Temporal is particularly impressive to mention in interviews - it provides saga orchestration as durable workflows written in plain code.

Compensating transactions are NOT rollbacks:

  • Interview-specific: A compensating transaction is a new forward transaction that semantically undoes the effect of a previous transaction. It does NOT restore the exact previous state. Refunding a payment creates a new credit transaction - it doesn't erase the original debit. Sending a cancellation email doesn't unsend the confirmation email. Unreserving inventory creates a new inventory adjustment record. Some operations cannot be perfectly compensated - you can't un-ship a physical package. Design business processes around this reality (return and refund policies instead of "undo shipment").

CQRS in Microservices

The pattern: Separate the write model (optimized for business rules and consistency) from read models (optimized for specific query patterns). The write side processes commands, stores events or updates the primary database, and publishes events. The read side consumes events and builds denormalized views shaped for specific query needs.

  • Interview-specific:
    • In microservices, CQRS solves the "how do I query across services?" problem. In a monolith, you join the orders table with the customers table. In microservices, orders and customers are in different databases in different services. With CQRS, the "order dashboard" read model consumes events from both OrderService and CustomerService and builds a denormalized view containing all the data needed for the dashboard - no cross-service calls at query time.
    • Multiple read models from the same events: the same OrderPlaced event might update an Elasticsearch index (for search), a Redis cache (for fast lookups), a PostgreSQL reporting table (for analytics), and a notification queue (for customer alerts).
    • The trade-off: eventual consistency. The read model lags behind the write model by the time it takes to process events. For most UIs, this lag (typically milliseconds to seconds) is invisible. For critical operations, read from the write model (the source of truth).

Event Sourcing in Microservices

The pattern: Instead of storing current state, store the complete sequence of events. Current state is derived by replaying events. Every state change is captured as an immutable event.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Event sourcing and microservices are a natural fit - events are both the internal state representation AND the inter-service communication mechanism. An event-sourced OrderService stores OrderCreated, ItemAdded, PaymentReceived events. It also publishes those same events for other services to consume.
    • Benefits in microservices: complete audit trail, temporal queries ("what was the order state at 3 PM yesterday?"), event replay (rebuild a service's state from scratch, populate a new service with historical data, recover from bugs).
    • Challenges: event schema evolution (old events in old format must remain readable), increased storage (events accumulate forever - use snapshots), eventual consistency (deriving state takes time), increased complexity (developers think in events, not CRUD).
    • When to use: domains with audit requirements (finance, healthcare, legal), domains where understanding "how did we get here?" matters, systems where event replay enables powerful capabilities (rebuilding projections, retroactive corrections).
    • When NOT to use: simple CRUD applications, domains where current state is all that matters, teams unfamiliar with event-driven thinking.

Data Consistency Patterns

Eventual Consistency: The default in microservices. After a change in one service, other services' views are eventually updated. The "eventually" window is typically milliseconds to seconds.

  • Interview-specific: Design the user experience for eventual consistency. After placing an order, the user sees "Order received - processing." The confirmation page doesn't need to show the invoice instantly. By the time the user navigates to their order history, the read model has been updated. This is a UX design challenge as much as a technical one.

Read-Your-Own-Writes Consistency: After a user writes data, THAT USER sees their own write immediately. Other users may see the update after replication lag.

Implementation: after a write, the response includes the written data. The client-side UI updates optimistically. Subsequent reads for that user are routed to the write-side store (or use a cache primed by the write) until the read model catches up.

Outbox Pattern: Ensures that a database write and an event publication are atomic - both happen or neither happens.

The problem: if OrderService writes to its database and then publishes a Kafka event, what if the application crashes between the database write and the Kafka publish? The order exists in the database but no event was published. Downstream services never learn about the order.

The solution: instead of publishing directly to Kafka, write the event to an "outbox" table in the same database within the same transaction as the business data. A separate process (Debezium CDC or a polling publisher) reads the outbox table and publishes to Kafka. Because the business data and the outbox entry are in the same transaction, they're guaranteed to be consistent.

  • Interview-specific: The Outbox Pattern is one of the most important patterns in microservices data management. It solves the dual-write problem (writing to two systems - database and message broker - without a distributed transaction). Know it cold, with implementation details. Debezium's outbox event router is the most common production implementation.

Transactional Outbox implementation options:

  1. CDC-based (Debezium): reads the database transaction log, captures outbox table changes, publishes to Kafka. No polling overhead. Near-real-time. The preferred approach.
  2. Polling publisher: a scheduled job queries the outbox table for unpublished events, publishes them, and marks them as published. Simpler to implement but adds latency (polling interval) and database load.
  1. Service Discovery and Registration

The Problem

In a microservices environment, services run on dynamic infrastructure. IP addresses change when containers restart. The number of instances changes with auto-scaling. Services need to find each other without hardcoded addresses.

Client-Side Discovery

The client queries a service registry, gets a list of available instances, and load-balances across them.

  • Interview-specific:
    • The service registry (Eureka, Consul) maintains a list of healthy instances. Each service instance registers itself on startup and sends heartbeats. If heartbeats stop, the instance is deregistered.
    • The client has a load balancing library that chooses which instance to call (round-robin, least connections, weighted).
    • Pros: the client has control over load balancing strategy. No additional network hop.
    • Cons: every client must implement discovery logic. Discovery library must exist for every programming language used.

Server-Side Discovery

The client sends requests to a load balancer or router. The router queries the service registry and forwards the request to a healthy instance. The client doesn't know about individual instances.

  • Interview-specific:
    • In Kubernetes, this is the default model. A Kubernetes Service provides a stable DNS name and IP. The client calls the Service name. kube-proxy routes to a healthy pod. The client doesn't need any discovery library.
    • AWS ALB + ECS: the load balancer routes to registered ECS tasks. Service discovery is handled by the platform.
    • Pros: clients are simple - just call a URL. Discovery logic is centralized.
    • Cons: the load balancer is an additional network hop and a potential bottleneck.

DNS-Based Discovery

Services are registered in DNS. The client resolves a hostname to get IP addresses of healthy instances.

  • Interview-specific: Kubernetes Services use DNS (CoreDNS). order-service.default.svc.cluster.local resolves to the Service's ClusterIP. Consul also provides DNS-based discovery. The challenge with DNS: TTL caching means stale entries persist for seconds to minutes. Fine for slowly changing environments, problematic when instances scale rapidly.

Service Mesh Discovery

A sidecar proxy (Envoy in Istio) handles service discovery transparently. The application calls http://order-service:8080 - the sidecar intercepts the call, resolves the destination using the mesh's service registry, and routes to a healthy instance.

  • Interview-specific: Service mesh discovery is the most transparent approach - zero application code changes. The sidecar handles discovery, load balancing, retries, circuit breaking, and mTLS. But it adds operational complexity (deploying and managing the mesh) and per-request latency (extra hop through the sidecar proxy).

Health Checks

  • Interview-specific: Service discovery is only useful if unhealthy instances are removed promptly. Every service must expose health endpoints:
    • Liveness: "Am I alive?" If no, kill and restart me. Should check only that the process isn't deadlocked. Should NOT check downstream dependencies.
    • Readiness: "Am I ready to serve traffic?" If no, remove me from the load balancer but don't kill me. Should check that startup is complete, connections are established, caches are warmed.
    • The liveness trap: if your liveness check depends on a downstream service (database, cache), and that dependency fails, ALL your instances will be killed simultaneously. The orchestrator restarts them. They can't connect to the database. They fail liveness again. They're killed again. Cascading restart loop. Liveness checks should be self-contained.
  1. API Gateway and Edge Services

API Gateway Pattern

What it is: A single entry point for all external client requests. Routes requests to appropriate backend services. Handles cross-cutting concerns at the edge.

Responsibilities:

  • Request routing (URL path → backend service)
  • Authentication and authorization (validate JWT tokens, API keys)
  • Rate limiting and throttling (protect backend from overload)
  • Request/response transformation (protocol translation, payload manipulation)
  • SSL/TLS termination
  • Response caching
  • Request logging and monitoring
  • CORS handling
  • API versioning
  • Interview-specific:
    • Without an API gateway, every client must know the addresses of all backend services, handle authentication separately for each, and deal with multiple protocols. The gateway provides a unified, simplified interface.
    • AWS API Gateway, Kong, Envoy, Spring Cloud Gateway, NGINX, Traefik, Ambassador are common choices.
    • API Gateway vs Service Mesh: the API gateway handles NORTH-SOUTH traffic (external clients → internal services). The service mesh handles EAST-WEST traffic (internal service → internal service). They complement each other, not replace each other.
    • Don't make the gateway too smart. It should route and handle cross-cutting concerns, not contain business logic. If the gateway is transforming request payloads, aggregating responses from multiple services, or making business decisions - it's becoming a monolithic bottleneck. Business logic belongs in services.

Backend for Frontend (BFF) Pattern

What it is: A separate API layer (or API gateway instance) for each client type. A mobile BFF optimizes for bandwidth (smaller payloads, fewer fields). A web BFF provides richer data. A third-party BFF may have different rate limits and authentication.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Without BFF: one API serves mobile, web, third-party, and internal clients. The mobile client receives fields it doesn't use (wasted bandwidth). The web client doesn't get enough data (requires additional calls). Each client type wants different aggregation, pagination, and response formats.
    • With BFF: each client type has an API layer shaped for its needs. The mobile BFF calls the same backend microservices but returns only the fields needed for mobile screens. It can also batch multiple service calls into one aggregated response to reduce round trips over mobile networks.
    • BFF ownership: the frontend team (mobile team, web team) owns their BFF. They can change the API contract between their client and their BFF without coordinating with backend teams. Backend services provide stable, general-purpose APIs. BFFs adapt those into client-specific interfaces.
    • Don't create too many BFFs. One per major client category (mobile, web, third-party) is typical. A separate BFF for every microservice defeats the purpose.

API Composition

What it is: Aggregating data from multiple microservices into a single response for the client.

  • Interview-specific:
    • In a monolith, a single query joins orders with customers with products. In microservices, these are in separate services. The API composer (which can be the BFF, the API gateway, or a dedicated composition service) calls OrderService, CustomerService, and ProductService, then assembles the combined response.
    • The risk: the composition layer makes synchronous calls to multiple services. If any service is slow, the entire response is slow. If any service is down, the entire response fails (unless you implement graceful degradation - return partial data with a "recommendations unavailable" placeholder).
    • Alternatives: CQRS read models (pre-join data asynchronously - faster queries, no runtime aggregation), GraphQL federation (each service provides its part of the graph, the gateway composes).
  1. Resilience Patterns for Microservices

Why Resilience Is Critical

In a monolith, a method call either succeeds or throws an exception. In microservices, a network call can: succeed, fail, time out, succeed slowly, return garbage, or hang forever. Network is unreliable. Services go down. Latency spikes. The question isn't IF failures happen but HOW the system behaves when they do.

Circuit Breaker Pattern

Three states:

  • Closed (normal): Requests flow through. Failures are counted. If failure rate exceeds threshold (e.g., 50% failures over last 10 seconds), circuit OPENS.
  • Open (failing fast): All requests immediately fail without calling the downstream service. Returns a fallback response or error. This protects both the caller (doesn't waste time waiting) and the failing service (gets time to recover without being hammered with requests). After a timeout (e.g., 30 seconds), circuit moves to HALF-OPEN.
  • Half-Open (testing): A limited number of test requests are allowed through. If they succeed, circuit CLOSES. If they fail, circuit OPENS again.
  • Interview-specific:
    • Without circuit breaker: Service A calls Service B. Service B is overloaded and taking 30 seconds to respond. Service A's threads are blocked waiting. Service A's thread pool fills up. Service A becomes unresponsive. Service C, which calls Service A, also becomes blocked. Cascading failure - one slow service takes down the entire system.
    • With circuit breaker: after detecting Service B is failing, Service A's circuit breaker opens. Calls to Service B fail immediately (milliseconds, not 30 seconds). Service A remains responsive using fallback logic. Service C is unaffected.
    • Resilience4j is the modern standard for circuit breaking. Hystrix is deprecated (Netflix moved to Resilience4j).
    • Configuration matters: failure rate threshold (50% is common), sliding window size (10 requests or 10 seconds), wait duration in open state (30 seconds), number of permitted calls in half-open state (5). These should be tuned based on the service's characteristics, not left at defaults.

Retry Pattern

What it is: Automatically retry failed requests that are likely to succeed on retry (transient failures: network blips, temporary 503s).

What NOT to retry: Non-transient failures (400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found, 401 Unauthorized). Retrying these wastes resources and doesn't fix anything.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Exponential backoff: wait 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s between retries. Gives the failing service time to recover.
    • Jitter: add random variation to the backoff. Without jitter, if 1000 clients all fail at the same time, they all retry at the same time (thundering herd). Jitter spreads retries across time.
    • Max retries: always set a limit (typically 3-5 retries). Infinite retries create infinite load on a struggling service.
    • Idempotency requirement: if a request times out, you don't know if the server processed it. Retrying a non-idempotent operation can cause double-processing (double charge, duplicate order). All operations that may be retried MUST be idempotent (sending the same request twice produces the same result as sending it once).
    • Retry budget: limit the total percentage of requests that are retries (e.g., 20%). If more than 20% of your traffic is retries, something is fundamentally wrong - you're amplifying the problem, not solving it.

Bulkhead Pattern

What it is: Isolate resources so one failing component doesn't consume all resources and bring down everything else.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Thread pool bulkhead: dedicate separate thread pools for calls to different downstream services. If InventoryService is slow and exhausts its thread pool, the thread pool for PaymentService is unaffected. PaymentService calls continue at normal speed.
    • Semaphore bulkhead: limit concurrent calls to a service without dedicated thread pools. Lighter weight but less isolation.
    • Connection pool bulkhead: separate database connection pools for different query patterns. A long-running analytics query shouldn't exhaust the connection pool needed for transaction processing.
    • The metaphor: bulkheads in a ship. If one compartment floods, the bulkheads prevent water from reaching other compartments. The ship stays afloat. Without bulkheads, one flood sinks the entire ship.

Timeout Pattern

Every external call must have a timeout. No exceptions.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Without timeouts: a call to a slow service hangs for minutes. The calling service's thread is blocked. Under load, all threads are blocked waiting for the slow service. The caller becomes unresponsive. Cascading failure.
    • Set timeouts at multiple levels: HTTP client connection timeout (how long to establish a connection - typically 1-3 seconds), read timeout (how long to wait for a response - based on expected response time), circuit breaker timeout (how long the circuit stays open), and database query timeout.
    • Timeout values should be based on percentile latency of the downstream service. If the downstream service's p99 latency is 500ms, a 2-second timeout is reasonable. A 30-second timeout means you'll hang for 30 seconds when the service is failing - too long.
    • Cascading timeouts: if Service A has a 5-second timeout calling Service B, and Service B has a 5-second timeout calling Service C, the total timeout from A's perspective can be 10+ seconds. Design timeouts as a budget that decreases at each hop.

Rate Limiting

What it is: Limit the number of requests a client (or a service) can make in a time window. Prevents a single client from overwhelming the service.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Token bucket algorithm: tokens are added at a fixed rate. Each request consumes a token. When the bucket is empty, requests are rejected (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests). Allows short bursts up to bucket capacity while enforcing an average rate.
    • Sliding window: count requests in a rolling time window. More precise than fixed windows (which have boundary edge cases).
    • Apply rate limiting at the API gateway for external clients. Apply per-service rate limiting for internal service-to-service calls to prevent one service from overwhelming another.
    • Include rate limit headers in responses (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset) so clients can back off gracefully.

Graceful Degradation

What it is: When a dependency fails, provide a reduced but functional experience rather than a complete failure.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Recommendation service down? Show "popular items" instead of personalized recommendations. The user still gets useful results.
    • Real-time pricing service slow? Show cached prices with a disclaimer "prices may not reflect latest changes."
    • Image processing service overloaded? Serve lower-resolution images or placeholders.
    • Search service unavailable? Show category browsing as an alternative navigation path.
    • The key: graceful degradation must be designed and coded in advance. It doesn't happen automatically. Each critical dependency needs a planned fallback behavior.

Chaos Engineering

What it is: Intentionally inject failures to verify system resilience. Don't wait for production failures to discover weaknesses.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Chaos Monkey (Netflix): randomly terminates instances. Verifies that auto-healing works and services handle instance loss gracefully.
    • Latency injection: add artificial delay to service calls. Verifies that timeouts and circuit breakers work correctly.
    • Dependency failure: block network access to a database or downstream service. Verifies that fallback logic activates.
    • Zone/region failure: simulate an entire AZ outage. Verifies that multi-AZ deployment and failover work correctly.
    • Run chaos experiments in production (carefully, with safeguards). Staging environments don't have real traffic patterns, real data volumes, or real operational procedures. The value of chaos engineering comes from validating real production resilience.
    • Tools: AWS Fault Injection Simulator, Gremlin, Chaos Mesh (Kubernetes), Chaos Monkey for Spring Boot.
  1. Observability in Microservices

Why Observability Is Critical

In a monolith, a request enters one process and you can follow it through the call stack. In microservices, a single user action can touch 5-15 services. Without observability, debugging a slow response or a failed request across 15 services with 50 instances is impossible. You're flying blind.

The Three Pillars

Structured Logging:

  • Interview-specific:
    • Every log line must include: timestamp, service name, trace/correlation ID, log level, message, relevant business context (user ID, order ID, request path).
    • Structured format (JSON, not free text). Bad: ERROR: Something failed for user 123. Good: {"timestamp":"2025-03-04T14:32:07Z","level":"ERROR","service":"order-service","traceId":"abc123","userId":"u-123","orderId":"o-456","message":"Payment gateway timeout","retryAttempt":2,"gatewayLatencyMs":5000}
    • Centralize logs: services write to stdout. Platform collects logs (Fluentd/Fluent Bit DaemonSet in Kubernetes). Shipped to centralized system (ELK, Grafana Loki, Datadog, CloudWatch Logs). Search across all services by correlation ID.
    • Log levels matter: DEBUG (verbose, disabled in production unless troubleshooting), INFO (normal business events), WARN (recoverable issues), ERROR (operation failures), FATAL (system-level failure).
    • What NOT to log: passwords, tokens, credit card numbers, PII without masking. Log enough to diagnose, never enough to compromise security.

Metrics:

  • Interview-specific:
    • RED method for services: Rate (requests/sec), Errors (error rate), Duration (latency distribution - p50, p95, p99). Every service should expose these.
    • USE method for resources: Utilization, Saturation, Errors. For CPU, memory, disk, network, thread pools, connection pools.
    • USE PERCENTILES, NOT AVERAGES for latency. An average of 100ms can hide a bimodal distribution where 95% of requests take 10ms but 5% take 2 seconds. p99 reveals the tail latency that affects real users.
    • Business metrics: orders/minute, payment success rate, cart abandonment rate, search result click-through rate. A system can be technically healthy while the business is failing. Monitor both.
    • Prometheus + Grafana is the open-source standard. Micrometer for application instrumentation. Datadog, New Relic, or CloudWatch for managed solutions.

Distributed Tracing:

  • Interview-specific:
    • A trace represents an end-to-end request journey. Each service creates a span - a named, timed operation within the trace. Spans have parent-child relationships forming a tree.
    • Example trace: User clicks checkout → API Gateway span (5ms) → OrderService span (50ms) → PaymentService span (200ms) → InventoryService span (30ms) → NotificationService span (10ms). Total: 295ms. You can see that PaymentService contributed 200ms - that's where to optimize.
    • Trace propagation: a unique trace ID is generated at the entry point. Each service passes it via HTTP headers (W3C Trace Context standard: traceparent header) or message metadata to downstream services. Every service includes the trace ID in its logs.
    • OpenTelemetry is the standard. Instrument once, export to Jaeger, Zipkin, Datadog, AWS X-Ray, or Grafana Tempo.
    • Sampling: tracing every request is expensive. Head-based sampling (decide at start - trace 10% of requests) is simple but misses interesting failures. Tail-based sampling (decide after completion - keep traces that were slow, errored, or unusual) is more expensive but captures what matters. Always trace errors and slow requests. Sample routine successes.

Correlation IDs

  • Interview-specific: The single most important observability practice. Generate a unique ID at the system entry point (API gateway). Propagate it through every synchronous call (HTTP header) and asynchronous message (message metadata). Include it in every log line and span. When an incident occurs, take the correlation ID from the user's error response, search all centralized logs, and see the complete request lifecycle across all services.

Health Endpoints and Probes

  • Interview-specific: Every microservice must expose:
    • /health/live - am I alive? Simple check that the process isn't deadlocked. Used by Kubernetes liveness probe. Failure → restart the pod.
    • /health/ready - am I ready to serve traffic? Check that startup is complete, dependencies are reachable, caches are warm. Used by Kubernetes readiness probe. Failure → remove from load balancer.
    • /health/details - internal endpoint showing status of every dependency (database: OK, Redis: OK, Kafka: DEGRADED). Used for dashboard and incident diagnosis. NOT exposed externally.

Alerting Philosophy

  • Interview-specific:
    • Alert on symptoms, not causes. Alert: "checkout error rate exceeded 5% for 3 minutes." Don't alert: "CPU at 85%." CPU at 85% might be fine. Checkout errors definitely aren't.
    • Every alert must be actionable. If the on-call engineer can't do anything at 3 AM, it should be a dashboard observation, not a page.
    • Severity levels: P1/Critical (customer-facing impact NOW - wake someone up), P2/Warning (trending toward trouble - investigate next business day), P3/Info (notable - review weekly).
    • Alert fatigue kills. 200 alerts/day means all are ignored. Ruthlessly prune. Every false positive erodes trust.
  1. Service Mesh

What a Service Mesh Is

A dedicated infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication. Implemented as a network of sidecar proxies deployed alongside every service instance. The application code makes simple network calls - the sidecar proxy handles everything else transparently.

Architecture

Data plane: Sidecar proxies (Envoy is dominant) deployed with every service. Intercepts all inbound and outbound network traffic. Handles load balancing, retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability.

Control plane: Manages and configures the sidecar proxies. Pushes configuration (routing rules, security policies) to all proxies. Handles certificate issuance and rotation. Istio's control plane is istiod. Linkerd's is its own lightweight control plane.

Capabilities

  • Interview-specific:

Traffic Management:

    • Canary deployments: route 5% of traffic to the new version, 95% to the old. Increase gradually as confidence grows.
    • A/B testing: route based on headers (user segment, feature flag, geography).
    • Traffic mirroring (shadowing): send a copy of production traffic to a test instance. The test instance processes it but responses are discarded. Validate new versions against real traffic without affecting users.
    • Fault injection: inject delays (test timeout/circuit breaker behavior) or errors (test fallback logic) into specific routes. Controlled chaos engineering.
    • Request retries and timeouts: configured centrally at the mesh level rather than in every service's code.

Security:

    • mTLS between all services: automatic certificate provisioning and rotation. Every service-to-service connection is encrypted and authenticated. Zero application code changes.
    • Authorization policies: "OrderService can call PaymentService. NotificationService cannot call PaymentService." Enforced at the network level by the proxy.
    • Zero-trust networking: don't trust traffic because it's "internal." Authenticate and authorize every request.

Observability:

    • Automatic metrics: request count, latency (p50, p95, p99), error rate for every service-to-service call. No instrumentation code needed.
    • Automatic distributed tracing: proxies inject and propagate trace headers.
    • Access logging: detailed logs for every request passing through the mesh.

When to Use a Service Mesh

  • Interview-specific:
    • Good fit: 20+ microservices, multiple teams, need consistent security (mTLS everywhere), need advanced traffic management (canary, A/B), want observability without modifying service code.
    • Bad fit: fewer than 10 services, team lacks Kubernetes expertise, simple request patterns where basic load balancing suffices, the operational overhead of managing the mesh exceeds the benefit.
    • Resource cost: each sidecar consumes memory (~50-100MB) and CPU. With 200 services, each having 3 replicas, that's 600 sidecar proxies consuming 30-60GB of memory collectively.
    • Istio vs Linkerd: Istio is more feature-rich (complex traffic management, policy engine, multi-cluster) but more complex to operate. Linkerd is lighter, simpler, and easier to start with. For most teams, Linkerd is sufficient. Istio is for complex multi-cluster, multi-mesh scenarios.
  1. Testing Microservices

The Testing Challenge

Testing a monolith: start the application, run tests against it. Testing microservices: Service A depends on B which depends on C and D. Do you start all four for every test? How do you test the integration between A and B without B's behavior being affected by C's bugs? How do you test in an environment that matches production's topology?

Unit Testing

Test individual components in isolation. Mock all external dependencies (database, other services, message brokers).

  • Interview-specific: Unit tests in microservices are the same as anywhere else. Test business logic independently of frameworks and infrastructure. Keep them fast (thousands of tests in seconds). High coverage of domain logic. Mock outgoing ports (repository interfaces, event publishers, external service clients).

Integration Testing

Test the service with real external dependencies (database, message broker) but without other microservices.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Testcontainers: spin up Docker containers of PostgreSQL, Kafka, Redis, Elasticsearch within the test. The test runs against real infrastructure, not mocks. Catches issues that unit tests with mocks miss: SQL syntax errors, serialization issues, transaction behavior.
    • Test the service's API: send HTTP requests to the service's endpoint and verify responses. Test database interactions. Test message consumption and publication.
    • These tests are slower than unit tests (seconds per test, not milliseconds) but much more valuable for catching real integration issues.

Contract Testing

The problem: Service A depends on Service B's API. Service B changes its API. Service A breaks in production. How do you catch this before deployment?

Consumer-Driven Contract Testing (Pact):

  • Interview-specific:
    • Consumer (Service A) writes a contract: "I expect Service B's GET /orders/{id} to return a JSON object with fields orderId (string), status (string), and totalAmount (number)."
    • The contract is shared with the provider (Service B).
    • Provider (Service B) verifies: "Does my API satisfy Service A's contract?" If yes, verification passes. If Service B removes the totalAmount field, verification fails - Service B knows it would break Service A before deploying.
    • This replaces expensive end-to-end integration tests. Each service is tested independently against contracts.
    • Consumer-driven means consumers define what they need, not what the provider offers. This prevents providers from accidentally breaking consumers.
    • Pact Broker: a central server that stores contracts and verification results. Shows which consumer-provider pairs are verified and which are broken. Integrates with CI/CD to prevent deployment of breaking changes.

Component Testing

Test a single service in isolation, with all external dependencies stubbed or containerized.

  • Interview-specific: Deploy the service, replace downstream services with stubs (WireMock for HTTP services, embedded Kafka with test topics for messaging), and test the service's behavior end-to-end within its boundary. This is faster and more reliable than full end-to-end tests while still validating the service's complete request processing pipeline.

End-to-End Testing (Use Sparingly)

Test the entire system with all services running. Deploy the full topology and run user scenarios.

  • Interview-specific:
    • End-to-end tests in microservices are: slow (deploying 20 services takes time), flaky (any service instability causes test failure), expensive (full environment for tests), and a deployment bottleneck (all teams share the test environment).
    • Use the testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, reliable, cheap), moderate integration and contract tests, few end-to-end tests for critical user journeys only.
    • Alternatives to extensive end-to-end testing: contract testing (verifies API compatibility), canary deployments (test in production with real traffic), monitoring and alerting (detect issues within minutes of deployment).
    • The pragmatic approach: "We run contract tests in CI for every service. End-to-end tests run nightly against a staging environment covering the top 10 critical user journeys. Canary deployments and monitoring catch everything else."

Testing in Production

  • Interview-specific:
    • Canary deployment: route a small percentage of real traffic to the new version. Monitor error rates and latency. If metrics degrade, roll back automatically. If stable, gradually increase traffic.
    • Feature flags: deploy code to production behind a flag. Enable for internal users first, then percentage rollout.
    • Synthetic monitoring: automated scripts that periodically execute critical user journeys against production. "Every 5 minutes, simulate a user searching for a product, adding it to cart, and checking out." Alert if any step fails.
    • Observability-driven development: deploy with confidence because you have the metrics, logs, and traces to detect and diagnose any issue within minutes.
  1. Deployment Strategies for Microservices

Rolling Deployment

Replace instances one at a time. Old and new versions run simultaneously during the rollout. No downtime.

  • Interview-specific:
    • In Kubernetes: Deployment resource with RollingUpdate strategy. maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0 - create one new pod, wait for it to be ready, terminate one old pod. Capacity never drops below desired replicas.
    • Requires backward compatibility between versions. The new version must work with the old version's data format, API contracts, and message schemas. If version 2 changes a database schema that version 1 can't read, rolling deployment fails.
    • Rollback: Kubernetes maintains revision history. kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous version instantly.

Blue-Green Deployment

Maintain two identical environments (blue and green). Blue is production. Deploy new version to green. Test green. Switch the load balancer from blue to green. Blue becomes the standby (instant rollback by switching back).

  • Interview-specific:
    • Pros: instant cutover, instant rollback, new version is fully tested in a production-like environment before receiving traffic.
    • Cons: requires double the infrastructure (both environments running simultaneously). Database migrations are tricky - both blue and green must work with the same database, which constrains schema changes.
    • Practical modification: blue-green at the service level (not the entire system). Switch individual services between blue and green independently.

Canary Deployment

Deploy the new version alongside the old. Route a small percentage of traffic (1%, 5%, 10%) to the new version. Monitor metrics (error rate, latency, business KPIs). If healthy, gradually increase traffic. If problematic, route all traffic back to the old version.
