  • Interview-specific:
    • This is the most common production deployment strategy for microservices. It provides real-world validation with limited blast radius.
    • Implementation: Kubernetes with Istio (traffic splitting via VirtualService), Argo Rollouts (automated canary with metric-based promotion), AWS App Mesh, Flagger.
    • Automated canary analysis: define success criteria (error rate < 1%, p99 latency < 500ms). The tool automatically promotes or rolls back based on real-time metrics. No human intervention needed for routine deployments.
    • Canary + feature flags: deploy the code with a feature flag disabled. Enable the flag for the canary traffic only. Separate code deployment from feature activation.

Feature Flag Deployment

Deploy code to 100% of instances. Activate features selectively using flags.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Decouples deployment from release. Code is deployed but inactive. Product managers can activate features for specific user segments without engineering involvement.
    • Flag types: release flags (gate new features - short-lived), experiment flags (A/B testing), ops flags (kill switches, circuit breakers), permission flags (feature access by user tier).
    • Flag hygiene: every flag has an owner and expiration date. Stale flags are tech debt. A system with 500 active flags is impossible to reason about. Regularly audit and remove completed flags.
    • Tools: LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Flagsmith, Togglz, ConfigCat.

GitOps Deployment

Git is the single source of truth. All deployments are triggered by Git commits. ArgoCD or Flux watches a Git repository and ensures the cluster state matches the repository state.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Workflow: developer merges PR → CI builds and pushes container image → CI updates the Kubernetes manifest in the GitOps repository (changes the image tag) → ArgoCD detects the change → ArgoCD applies the new manifest to the cluster → deployment happens.
    • Audit trail: every deployment is a Git commit with author, timestamp, and change details. "Who deployed what and when?" is answered by git log.
    • Rollback: revert the Git commit. ArgoCD applies the reverted state. The cluster returns to the previous version.
    • Drift detection: if someone manually changes the cluster (kubectl edit), ArgoCD detects the drift and can either alert or auto-revert.
  1. Microservices Security

Authentication and Authorization

  • Interview-specific:
    • API Gateway authentication: The gateway validates the authentication token (JWT, OAuth2 access token) on every request. Invalid tokens are rejected at the gateway - backend services never see unauthenticated requests.
    • Token propagation: After validation, the gateway forwards the token (or extracted claims) to backend services via headers. Each service makes its own authorization decisions based on the claims ("does this user have permission to cancel this order?").
    • JWT (JSON Web Tokens): Stateless tokens containing claims (user ID, roles, permissions, expiration). Signed by the auth server (asymmetric keys - auth server signs with private key, services verify with public key). Services can verify tokens without calling the auth server - this is critical for avoiding the auth server becoming a bottleneck.
    • JWT limitations: Tokens can't be revoked before expiry (they're stateless - no central tracking). Mitigations: short expiry times (15 minutes), token blacklist for critical revocations (partially defeats statelessness), refresh token rotation.
    • OAuth2 scopes and roles for service-to-service: Services authenticate to each other using client credentials grant. Each service has its own client ID and secret. Scopes limit what a service can do ("inventory-service can call order-service.read but not order-service.write").

Service-to-Service Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • mTLS (Mutual TLS): Both caller and callee present certificates. Proves identity in both directions. Encrypts all traffic. In a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd), mTLS is automatic - the mesh provisions certificates for every service, rotates them regularly, and terminates/originates TLS in the sidecar proxy. Zero application code changes.
    • Without a service mesh: Use a certificate authority (HashiCorp Vault) to issue certificates to services. Each service presents its certificate on connection. More operational overhead than mesh-managed mTLS.
    • Network policies (Kubernetes): Restrict which services can communicate. "Only the order-service pod can connect to payment-service on port 8080." Defense in depth - even if mTLS is compromised, network policies provide an additional layer.
    • Zero-trust within the cluster: Don't trust traffic because it's "internal." The network perimeter is not a security boundary. Every service-to-service call should be authenticated and authorized.

Secrets Management

  • Interview-specific:
    • Never hardcode secrets. Never store secrets in environment variables baked into container images. Never commit secrets to Git.
    • Use a secrets manager: HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager. Secrets are retrieved at runtime.
    • Dynamic secrets (Vault's killer feature): instead of static database passwords, Vault generates a unique, short-lived database credential for each service instance. Credentials expire automatically. If compromised, blast radius is limited to one instance for a short time. No manual rotation needed.
    • In Kubernetes: External Secrets Operator (syncs secrets from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager into Kubernetes Secrets), Vault Agent sidecar (injects secrets into pods), Sealed Secrets (encrypted secrets in Git, decrypted in-cluster).

API Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Input validation: never trust client input. Validate every field against expected format, length, and type. Reject invalid requests early.
    • Rate limiting at the API gateway: prevent abuse and DDoS.
    • CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing): configure which domains can call your API from a browser. Don't use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * in production.
    • SQL injection prevention: use parameterized queries. ORMs do this by default. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.
    • Dependency scanning: automatically scan application dependencies for known vulnerabilities (Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check). Fail the CI build if critical vulnerabilities are found.
    • Container image scanning: scan images for OS-level vulnerabilities (Trivy, ECR scanning).
  1. Microservices Anti-Patterns (Deep Dive)

Distributed Monolith

  • Interview-specific: The most common anti-pattern. Symptoms:
    • All services must be deployed together for any change to work
    • Services share a database
    • Changing one service requires changes in multiple other services
    • A single service failure cascades to all others
    • Integration tests require all services to run

How to detect: draw the dependency graph. If it's a tangled web where every service depends on every other service, you have a distributed monolith. If services can't be deployed, tested, or scaled independently, you have a distributed monolith.

How to fix: identify true bounded contexts, introduce event-driven communication to reduce synchronous coupling, split shared databases, define clear API contracts between services.

Chatty Services

  • Interview-specific: A single user action requires dozens of synchronous inter-service calls. "To display the product page, we call ProductService, PricingService, InventoryService, ReviewService, RecommendationService, and PromotionService - all synchronously."

Problems: latency adds up (6 calls � 50ms = 300ms minimum), reliability decreases (if any call fails, the page fails - 99.9%^6 = 99.4% availability), and debugging is complex.

Solutions: consolidate services that are always called together (the boundary may be wrong), use BFF pattern to aggregate and cache, use CQRS read models (pre-aggregate data so one query serves the page), use asynchronous data replication (each service has a local copy of the data it needs).

Shared Library Hell

  • Interview-specific: A common shared library (company-utils, common-domain) used by all services. When the library is updated, all services must update and redeploy. This creates hidden coupling - services aren't truly independently deployable.

The rule: share infrastructure libraries cautiously (HTTP client, logging, metrics - these change rarely). NEVER share domain libraries across services (domain models should be specific to each bounded context).

Duplication is preferable to coupling. If two services need a "Money" value object, each service defines its own. Yes, the code is duplicated. But the services are decoupled - changing one service's Money class doesn't require redeploying the other.

God Service

  • Interview-specific: One service that knows too much and does too much. It orchestrates everything, contains business logic for multiple domains, and every request flows through it. It's a monolith with extra network hops.

Often emerges when: the API gateway absorbs business logic, or a saga orchestrator grows beyond orchestration into processing, or a "core" service was never decomposed.

Fix: identify the distinct business capabilities within the god service. Extract them into separate services. The god service becomes a thin orchestrator or is eliminated entirely.

Premature Decomposition

  • Interview-specific: Decomposing into microservices before the domain is understood. Wrong boundaries are drawn. Constant refactoring across service boundaries (10x harder than refactoring within a monolith). The team spends more time fighting distributed systems complexity than delivering features.

The rule: start monolith (or modular monolith). Prove the boundaries by building and operating the modules. Extract to services only when: the team is large enough to benefit from independent deployment, operational maturity exists, and the boundaries have been validated.

Lack of Observability

  • Interview-specific: Building microservices without centralized logging, distributed tracing, and metrics. When something goes wrong (and it will), nobody can diagnose the issue. Debugging becomes "read the logs of each service and try to correlate timestamps manually." Incidents take hours instead of minutes to resolve.

Observability is not an afterthought - it's a prerequisite for microservices. Build it before or alongside the first service, not after you have 20 services in production.

  1. Microservices Migration (Monolith to Microservices)

When to Migrate

  • Interview-specific: Migrate when the monolith is actively harming the business: deployment coupling is slowing delivery, team coordination overhead is unsustainable, scaling constraints are hitting business limits, or technology lock-in prevents necessary innovation.

Don't migrate because "microservices are better." Don't migrate because "we want to use Kubernetes." The migration must be justified by specific, measurable business problems.

Strangler Fig Pattern

  • Interview-specific: The gold standard for migration. Named after strangler fig trees that grow around a host tree and gradually replace it.

Steps:

    1. Place a facade (API gateway, reverse proxy) in front of the monolith
    2. Identify one well-bounded feature to migrate first (choose something with clear inputs/outputs and minimal entanglement)
    3. Build the new service
    4. Route traffic for that feature to the new service
    5. Verify correctness (run both old and new in parallel, compare outputs)
    6. Decommission the feature in the monolith
    7. Repeat for the next feature

The monolith shrinks gradually. At no point is there a big-bang cutover. Risk is bounded to one feature at a time. If the new service fails, route traffic back to the monolith instantly.

What to Extract First

  • Interview-specific:
    • Start with the EASIEST service to extract, not the most important. Build team confidence and operational muscle.
    • Good first candidates: a service with clear boundaries and minimal dependencies on other parts of the monolith. A read-heavy module that can be fronted by a separate API. A new feature that hasn't been built yet - build it as a service from the start.
    • Bad first candidates: the deeply entangled core business logic. The module that shares tables with everything else. The module with circular dependencies.

Database Decomposition During Migration

  • Interview-specific: This is the hardest part. The shared database is the strongest coupling in most monoliths.

Incremental approach:

    1. Identify which tables belong to the service being extracted
    2. Create APIs in the new service for accessing that data
    3. Modify the monolith to call the new service's API instead of querying the tables directly
    4. Once the monolith no longer accesses those tables directly, move the tables to the new service's database
    5. Use CDC (Debezium) to keep data synchronized during the transition period if needed

The biggest challenges: shared tables (both modules write to the same table - must be split), foreign keys across module boundaries (must be replaced with API calls or events), views and stored procedures that span modules (must be decomposed or replicated), and application code that assumes joins across domain boundaries.

Parallel Run Pattern

Run both the monolith and the new service simultaneously for the same functionality. Compare outputs. Log discrepancies. Fix until outputs match consistently. Then switch traffic.

  • Interview-specific: Expensive (double compute) but invaluable for high-stakes migrations. Financial institutions and healthcare systems use this to ensure zero behavioral regression during migration.
  1. Evolutionary Architecture for Microservices

Fitness Functions

Automated checks that verify the architecture maintains desired properties as it evolves.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Dependency fitness functions: ArchUnit tests that verify module boundaries within a service, ensuring domain layer doesn't depend on infrastructure layer.
    • Inter-service coupling fitness function: automatically analyze service dependency graphs. Alert if the number of synchronous dependencies for any service exceeds a threshold (e.g., more than 3 synchronous downstream dependencies suggests the service boundary is wrong).
    • Performance fitness functions: automated performance tests in CI that fail if p99 latency exceeds SLA.
    • Security fitness functions: automated vulnerability scanning that fails the build on critical findings.

Evolutionary Service Boundaries

  • Interview-specific:
    • Boundaries will be wrong initially. Design for change - make it easy to merge two services (if the boundary was too granular) or split one service (if it grew too large).
    • Signals that a boundary is wrong: two services always change together, cross-service calls dominate request paths, one service is a "god service" with too many responsibilities, teams consistently need to coordinate across service boundaries for routine changes.
    • Merging services is underrated. If two services are always deployed together, always called together, and owned by the same team - merge them. Fewer services means less operational overhead. The goal isn't maximum services - it's optimal services.

Summary - Interview Signal Map

Topic

Junior Signal

Senior/Architect Signal

When to use

"Microservices are always better"

Articulates when NOT to use, understands the spectrum (modular monolith → services)

Decomposition

"One service per entity"

Decomposes by business capability, uses DDD bounded contexts, avoids premature decomposition

Communication

"Use REST everywhere"

Chooses sync/async per interaction, understands event types, uses gRPC for performance

Data

"Share the database"

Database-per-service, saga pattern, outbox pattern, CQRS for cross-service queries

Resilience

"Add retries"

Circuit breaker + bulkhead + timeout + rate limiting + graceful degradation, chaos engineering

Observability

"Check the logs"

Structured logging + metrics (RED/USE) + distributed tracing + correlation IDs, alerting philosophy

Testing

"Run end-to-end tests"

Testing pyramid: unit → integration (Testcontainers) → contract (Pact) → limited E2E, testing in production

Deployment

"Deploy manually"

Canary + feature flags + GitOps, automated rollback, zero-downtime migrations

Security

"Firewall at the edge"

Zero-trust, mTLS (service mesh), JWT propagation, dynamic secrets, network policies

Anti-patterns

Not mentioned

Distributed monolith, chatty services, shared library hell, god service, premature decomposition

Migration

"Rewrite from scratch"

Strangler fig, incremental extraction, database decomposition, parallel run

Team structure

Not mentioned

Conway's Law, Team Topologies, service ownership model, platform team enabling stream-aligned teams

 

 

 

Security Excellence

  1. Security Fundamentals and Mindset

What Interviewers Are Testing

They want to see that security isn't an afterthought you bolt on - it's woven into how you think about every design decision. They're looking for threat awareness, defense-in-depth thinking, understanding of the shared responsibility model, and the ability to balance security with usability and velocity.

Core Security Principles

CIA Triad:

Confidentiality: Data is accessible only to authorized parties. Achieved through: encryption (at rest and in transit), access controls (RBAC, ABAC), data classification and masking. Violation example: a database breach exposing customer records, an employee accessing salary data they shouldn't see, an API returning sensitive fields to unauthorized callers.

Integrity: Data is accurate and hasn't been tampered with. Achieved through: checksums, digital signatures, write-access controls, audit trails, immutable logs. Violation example: an attacker modifying a bank transaction amount in transit, an insider changing audit logs to cover tracks, a corrupted database record going undetected.

Availability: Systems are accessible when needed. Achieved through: redundancy, DDoS protection, rate limiting, capacity planning, disaster recovery. Violation example: a DDoS attack taking down a website, a ransomware attack encrypting a hospital's systems, a misconfigured deployment crashing all instances.

  • Interview-specific: Every security discussion should map back to CIA. When evaluating a threat, ask: "Does this threaten confidentiality, integrity, or availability - or some combination?" This framing demonstrates structured thinking.

Beyond CIA - Additional Principles:

Non-repudiation: A party cannot deny having performed an action. Achieved through digital signatures, audit logs, and transaction records. Critical for financial systems, legal compliance, and regulatory audits.

Least Privilege: Every entity (user, service, process) gets the minimum permissions needed to perform its function - nothing more. An application that only reads from S3 should not have S3 write permissions. A developer who only needs access to staging should not have production access.

  • Interview-specific: Least privilege is the single most impactful security practice. Most breaches exploit excessive permissions - an attacker compromises a low-level account and finds it has admin access to everything. Implementing least privilege is tedious (fine-grained permissions, regular access reviews, just-in-time access) but dramatically reduces blast radius.

Defense in Depth: Multiple layers of security controls. No single layer is trusted to be sufficient. If the firewall is bypassed, authentication catches it. If authentication is bypassed, authorization catches it. If authorization is bypassed, encryption protects the data. If encryption is compromised, monitoring detects the anomaly.

  • Interview-specific: When designing any system, describe at least 3-4 security layers. "We have a WAF at the edge, authentication at the API gateway, authorization in each service, encryption at rest in the database, and audit logging for all data access." This defense-in-depth articulation is what interviewers listen for.

Fail Secure (Fail Closed): When a security mechanism fails, default to denying access rather than granting it. If the authorization service is unavailable, reject the request - don't allow it through. If a JWT can't be validated, reject it - don't assume it's valid.

Separation of Duties: No single person or system should have complete control over a critical process. The person who writes code shouldn't approve their own deployment to production. The person who creates a financial transaction shouldn't be the same person who approves it. This prevents both malicious actions and honest mistakes.

Security by Design vs Security by Obscurity: Design systems that are secure even if the attacker knows the architecture. Relying on hidden URLs, undocumented APIs, or secret algorithms is security by obscurity - it fails the moment an attacker discovers the secret. True security withstands public scrutiny (Kerckhoffs's principle).

Threat Modeling

What it is: A structured process for identifying security threats, vulnerabilities, and mitigations BEFORE building the system - not after a breach.

STRIDE Model (Microsoft):

  • Spoofing: Pretending to be someone else. User impersonation, IP spoofing, phished credentials. Mitigation: strong authentication (MFA), certificate-based identity, anti-phishing measures.
  • Tampering: Modifying data without authorization. Man-in-the-middle attacks modifying API payloads, SQL injection changing database records, configuration file manipulation. Mitigation: TLS for data in transit, input validation, digital signatures, integrity checks.
  • Repudiation: Denying having performed an action. A user claims they didn't authorize a transaction. Mitigation: comprehensive audit logging, digital signatures, non-repudiation mechanisms.
  • Information Disclosure: Exposing data to unauthorized parties. Database breaches, verbose error messages leaking stack traces, API responses including unnecessary sensitive fields. Mitigation: encryption, access controls, data minimization, proper error handling.
  • Denial of Service: Making the system unavailable. DDoS attacks, resource exhaustion, algorithmic complexity attacks. Mitigation: rate limiting, auto-scaling, CDN/DDoS protection, input validation.
  • Elevation of Privilege: Gaining unauthorized permissions. SQL injection leading to admin access, exploiting a misconfigured IAM role, insecure direct object reference (IDOR) allowing access to other users' data. Mitigation: least privilege, input validation, authorization checks at every layer.
  • Interview-specific: When asked "how would you secure this system?" use STRIDE as a framework. Walk through each threat category for the system being discussed. This systematic approach impresses interviewers far more than ad-hoc security suggestions.

Threat Modeling Process:

  1. Decompose the system: Draw data flow diagrams showing components, data stores, external entities, and trust boundaries (where data crosses from one trust level to another - e.g., from internet to internal network, from application to database).
  2. Identify threats: For each component and data flow, apply STRIDE. "What could be spoofed? What data could be tampered with?"
  3. Assess risk: Likelihood � Impact. A common vulnerability in a public-facing service is high risk. A theoretical vulnerability in an air-gapped internal tool is low risk.
  4. Mitigate or accept: For each threat, either implement a mitigation (encryption, validation, access control) or explicitly accept the risk (documented decision with business justification).
  • Interview-specific: Mention that you perform threat modeling during design phase, not after implementation. "We threat-model during architecture review. Every new service goes through STRIDE analysis before development starts." This signals mature security practice.

Attack Surface Management

Attack surface: The sum of all points where an attacker can try to enter or extract data from a system. Every API endpoint, every open port, every user input field, every third-party integration, every exposed service, every dependency with known vulnerabilities.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Minimize attack surface: close unused ports, remove unnecessary endpoints, disable debug modes in production, decommission unused services, restrict network access to only what's needed.
    • Know your attack surface: maintain an inventory of all public endpoints, all external dependencies, all data flows crossing trust boundaries. What you don't know about can hurt you.
    • Shadow IT and shadow APIs: endpoints created by teams without going through security review. Periodic scanning (API discovery tools, network scanning) detects these.
  1. Authentication and Identity Management

Authentication Fundamentals

Authentication (AuthN): Verifying WHO someone is. "Are you really Alice?" Authorization (AuthZ): Verifying WHAT someone can do. "Alice is authenticated, but can she delete this resource?"

These are separate concerns and should be implemented separately.

Authentication Factors

Something you know: Password, PIN, security questions. Weakest factor - can be phished, guessed, or stolen. Passwords are the most common attack vector.

Something you have: Physical token, mobile phone (SMS code, authenticator app), hardware security key (YubiKey, FIDO2). Stronger - attacker needs physical access.

Something you are: Biometrics - fingerprint, face recognition, iris scan. Strongest for identifying a specific individual but can't be changed if compromised (you can change a password, you can't change your fingerprint).

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): Combine two or more factors. Password (know) + authenticator app (have). Even if the password is stolen, the attacker needs the phone.

  • Interview-specific: MFA should be MANDATORY for: all administrative access, all production system access, all developer accounts, all API access to sensitive data. The most common breach pattern is compromised credentials without MFA. "We enforce MFA for all human access to any system with access to production data or customer data" is the expected answer.

Modern Authentication Protocols

OAuth 2.0:

An AUTHORIZATION framework (not authentication - though often confused). Delegates access without sharing credentials. The user authorizes an application to access their resources without giving the application their password.

Key roles:

  • Resource Owner: the user who owns the data
  • Client: the application requesting access
  • Authorization Server: issues tokens after verifying the user's identity
  • Resource Server: the API hosting the protected resources

Grant types:

  • Authorization Code (with PKCE): the standard for web and mobile apps. User is redirected to the authorization server, authenticates, consents, and is redirected back with an authorization code. The client exchanges the code for tokens. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) prevents code interception attacks - mandatory for public clients (mobile apps, SPAs).
  • Client Credentials: service-to-service authentication. No user involved. The client authenticates with its own credentials and receives a token. Used for backend microservice communication.
  • Device Code: for devices without a browser (smart TV, CLI tool). Device displays a code, user enters it on another device.
  • Refresh Token: a long-lived token used to obtain new access tokens without re-authentication. Stored securely, rotated on use. If compromised, revoke it and all associated access tokens are invalidated at next refresh.
  • Interview-specific: "When should you use which grant type?" Authorization Code + PKCE for any user-facing application (web, mobile, SPA). Client Credentials for service-to-service. Device Code for browserless devices. NEVER use the Implicit grant (deprecated - tokens in URL fragments, vulnerable to interception) or Resource Owner Password Credentials (anti-pattern - client sees user's password).

OpenID Connect (OIDC):

An AUTHENTICATION layer built on top of OAuth 2.0. OAuth tells you "this client is authorized to access this resource." OIDC tells you "this user is Alice, she has email alice@example.com, and she authenticated 5 minutes ago."

OIDC adds: an ID token (a JWT containing user identity claims), a UserInfo endpoint, and standardized claim names (sub, email, name, picture).

  • Interview-specific: OAuth 2.0 = authorization (what can this client do?). OIDC = authentication (who is this user?). Use OIDC when you need to know who the user is (login, user profile). Use OAuth 2.0 when you need to delegate access to resources (API access on behalf of a user).

SAML 2.0:

An older XML-based standard for enterprise Single Sign-On (SSO). Still widely used in enterprise environments. Exchanges authentication and authorization data between an Identity Provider (IdP) and a Service Provider (SP).

  • Interview-specific: SAML is the enterprise SSO standard, especially for legacy systems. OIDC is the modern alternative and is simpler. New systems should use OIDC. Support SAML when integrating with enterprise customers who require it.

JWT (JSON Web Tokens) Deep Dive

Structure: Three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: Header.Payload.Signature

Header: Algorithm and token type. {"alg":"RS256","typ":"JWT"} Payload: Claims - key-value pairs containing user identity, permissions, expiration. {"sub":"user-123","email":"alice@example.com","roles":["admin"],"iat":1709568000,"exp":1709568900} Signature: Created by signing Header + Payload with a secret (HMAC) or private key (RSA/ECDSA). Proves the token hasn't been tampered with.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Symmetric vs Asymmetric signing: HMAC (HS256) uses a shared secret - both the issuer and verifier know the secret. RSA/ECDSA (RS256, ES256) uses a key pair - the issuer signs with a private key, verifiers validate with the public key. In microservices, ALWAYS use asymmetric signing. The auth server holds the private key. Services verify with the public key (fetched from the JWKS endpoint). No service needs the private key - smaller blast radius.
    • JWT validation steps: Check the signature (verify with public key). Check the expiration (exp claim). Check the issuer (iss claim - is it from our auth server?). Check the audience (aud claim - is this token intended for our service?). Check the not-before time (nbf claim). Only after ALL checks pass should the token be trusted.
    • JWT limitations: Tokens are stateless - they can't be revoked before expiry. If a user's account is disabled, their existing JWT is still valid until it expires. Mitigations: short expiry times (15 minutes), token blacklist (a revocation list checked on each request - partially defeats statelessness but necessary for critical revocations), or use opaque tokens with introspection for high-security operations.
    • Never store sensitive data in JWTs. The payload is Base64-encoded, NOT encrypted. Anyone who intercepts the token can read the payload. Don't put passwords, full credit card numbers, or highly sensitive data in JWT claims.
    • Token storage on the client: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies for web applications (prevents XSS from accessing the token, prevents CSRF). NOT localStorage (accessible to any JavaScript, vulnerable to XSS). For mobile apps, use the OS-level secure storage (Keychain on iOS, Keystore on Android).

Session Management

Stateful sessions: Server stores session data (user identity, permissions, state) in memory or a session store (Redis, database). Client holds a session ID (in a cookie). Each request sends the session ID; the server looks up the session. Revocation is instant (delete the session). But requires a centralized session store - adds infrastructure and is a potential bottleneck/single point of failure.

Stateless sessions (JWT): Token contains all session data. No server-side storage. Each request sends the full token. Server validates the token without external lookups. Scales horizontally trivially (any instance can validate any token). But revocation is hard (see JWT limitations above).

  • Interview-specific: The choice between stateful and stateless sessions is a trade-off between scalability and control. For most web applications, JWTs with short expiry + refresh tokens + a blacklist for critical revocations is the standard approach. For ultra-high-security applications (banking, healthcare), stateful sessions with a distributed session store (Redis Cluster) may be preferred for instant revocation capability.

Passwordless Authentication

  • Interview-specific: The trend is moving AWAY from passwords entirely.
    • Magic links: Email a login link with a time-limited token. User clicks the link, is authenticated. No password to forget, phish, or breach.
    • WebAuthn / FIDO2: Browser-based authentication using hardware security keys or platform authenticators (fingerprint, face recognition). Phishing-resistant (the authentication is bound to the origin - can't be replayed on a different site). The strongest authentication method available.
    • Passkeys: Consumer-friendly implementation of WebAuthn. Stored in the device's secure enclave, synced across devices via cloud (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager). Replaces passwords with biometric + cryptographic authentication. Apple, Google, and Microsoft all support passkeys.

Single Sign-On (SSO)

  • Interview-specific:
    • User authenticates once with the Identity Provider (IdP). The IdP issues tokens. All applications trust the IdP and accept its tokens. User doesn't re-authenticate when switching between applications.
    • Enterprise SSO: Okta, Azure AD (Entra ID), Auth0, Keycloak. Centralized identity management, user provisioning, MFA enforcement, audit logging.
    • SSO security considerations: the IdP is a high-value target - if compromised, all applications are compromised. Protect with strong MFA, monitoring for anomalous logins, and conditional access policies (block logins from unusual locations or devices).
  1. Authorization and Access Control

Authorization Models

RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):

Permissions are assigned to roles. Users are assigned to roles. User gets the union of all permissions from all assigned roles.

Example: ADMIN role has [create, read, update, delete]. EDITOR role has [create, read, update]. VIEWER role has [read]. User Alice is assigned ADMIN. User Bob is assigned VIEWER.

  • Interview-specific:
    • RBAC is the most common model. Simple to understand, implement, and audit.
    • Limitations: role explosion - in complex organizations, the number of roles proliferates (ADMIN_FINANCE_EU, ADMIN_FINANCE_US, EDITOR_MARKETING_APAC...). Hundreds of roles become unmanageable.
    • Hierarchical RBAC: roles inherit from other roles. ADMIN inherits all EDITOR permissions. EDITOR inherits all VIEWER permissions. Reduces duplication.
    • Use RBAC when: the number of distinct permission patterns is manageable (under ~50 roles), access decisions are based primarily on the user's job function, and the model doesn't need to consider resource-specific attributes.

ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):

Access decisions based on attributes of the user, the resource, the action, and the environment.

Example: "A user can edit a document IF the user.department == document.department AND document.status == 'DRAFT' AND current_time is within business_hours AND user.clearance_level >= document.classification_level."

  • Interview-specific:
    • ABAC is more flexible than RBAC. It handles complex, context-dependent access decisions that would require hundreds of roles in RBAC.
    • Policies are expressed in a policy language. "If user.role == 'doctor' AND patient.assignedDoctor == user.id AND request.action == 'view' AND resource.type == 'medical_record', THEN allow."
    • Tools: Open Policy Agent (OPA) - a general-purpose policy engine. Define policies in Rego language. Query OPA from any service for authorization decisions. OPA can run as a sidecar, as a library, or as a centralized service.
    • Use ABAC when: access decisions depend on resource attributes (ownership, classification), environmental conditions (time, location, IP), or relationships (user assigned to patient, manager of team), and RBAC role explosion is a problem.

ReBAC (Relationship-Based Access Control):

Access decisions based on relationships between entities. "User can edit Document if User is Owner of Document, or User is Member of Team that owns Document, or User is Admin of Organization that contains Team that owns Document."

  • Interview-specific:
    • Modeled as a graph of relationships. Google Zanzibar (paper) is the foundational model. Open-source implementations: SpiceDB, OpenFGA (Auth0), Ory Keto.
    • Natural for applications with ownership, sharing, and hierarchical permissions. Google Drive's sharing model is ReBAC - you can share with specific users, with a group, or make something public. Permissions propagate through relationships.
    • Use ReBAC when: the domain has rich relationships between users and resources (collaboration tools, file sharing, multi-tenant SaaS with team/organization hierarchies).

Policy-Based Access Control:

Externalize authorization decisions from application code into a dedicated policy engine.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Without a policy engine: authorization logic is scattered across every service's code. if (user.role == "admin") { allow(); } is duplicated and inconsistent across 20 services. Changing a policy requires changing code in multiple services.
    • With a policy engine (OPA): authorization logic is centralized in policies. Services call the policy engine: "Can user X perform action Y on resource Z?" The engine evaluates policies and returns allow/deny. Changing a policy doesn't require changing any service code.
    • OPA can be deployed as a sidecar per service (low latency, no network dependency) or as a centralized service (easier management, single point of failure).

Fine-Grained Authorization Patterns

Resource-level authorization: Can this user access this specific resource? Not just "can user view orders" but "can user view ORDER-12345?" Check ownership, team membership, or explicit sharing.

Field-level authorization: Different users see different fields of the same resource. A regular user sees their own profile (name, email). An HR admin sees the same profile plus salary, performance reviews. An auditor sees access logs. Implement at the API layer - filter response fields based on the requester's permissions.

Operation-level authorization: Different users can perform different operations. A viewer can GET. An editor can GET and PUT. An admin can GET, PUT, and DELETE.

  • Interview-specific: In microservices, authorization should happen at the service level, not just at the gateway. The gateway handles authentication (is the token valid?). Each service handles authorization (does this authenticated user have permission for this specific operation on this specific resource?). This prevents bypasses where a service is called directly (bypassing the gateway) and has no authorization checks.
  1. Network Security

Network Security Layers

Perimeter Security (First Layer):

  • Firewall: Controls traffic based on rules (source IP, destination IP, port, protocol). Network firewalls protect the network boundary. Host firewalls (Security Groups in cloud) protect individual instances.
  • WAF (Web Application Firewall): Inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests against rules. Blocks: SQL injection attempts, cross-site scripting (XSS), malformed requests, known attack patterns, bad bots, request floods from specific IPs.
  • DDoS Protection: Absorb or deflect volumetric attacks. Cloud-native (AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection) or CDN-based (Cloudflare, Akamai). Absorbs attack traffic at the edge before it reaches your infrastructure. Shield Standard (free, protects against common attacks) vs Shield Advanced (paid, larger attacks, 24/7 response team, cost protection).
  • CDN as security layer: CDN edge locations absorb attack traffic, terminate TLS, and apply WAF rules before traffic reaches origin servers. The origin's IP is never exposed directly.

Network Segmentation (Second Layer):

  • VPC and subnets: Public subnets (load balancers, bastion hosts - reachable from internet). Private subnets (application servers, databases - NOT reachable from internet, access through NAT gateway for outbound only).
  • Micro-segmentation: Network policies that restrict traffic between individual services. Service A can talk to Service B on port 8080 but NOT to Service C. In Kubernetes: Network Policies with Calico or Cilium. In cloud: Security Groups with explicit allow rules.
  • DMZ (Demilitarized Zone): A network segment between the public internet and the internal network. External-facing services live in the DMZ. They can communicate with internal services through tightly controlled firewall rules. The internal network is never directly exposed.
  • Interview-specific: Always describe network security as layers. "Internet traffic hits our CDN/WAF first. Legitimate traffic reaches the load balancer in the public subnet. The load balancer forwards to application instances in private subnets. Application instances access the database in an isolated data subnet. Each layer has its own firewall rules allowing only the minimum necessary traffic."

Transport Security (Third Layer):

  • TLS (Transport Layer Security): Encrypts all data in transit. HTTPS (HTTP over TLS) for all external communication. TLS 1.2 is the minimum acceptable version. TLS 1.3 is preferred (faster handshake, stronger ciphers, removes vulnerable algorithms).
  • Certificate management: Use certificates from trusted Certificate Authorities (CA). Automate certificate renewal (Let's Encrypt, AWS ACM). Monitor certificate expiry - an expired certificate causes outages. Certificate pinning (mobile apps) prevents man-in-the-middle attacks with fraudulent certificates.
  • mTLS (Mutual TLS): Both client and server present certificates. Proves identity in both directions. Standard for service-to-service communication in zero-trust environments. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles mTLS automatically.

Zero Trust Architecture

Core principle: "Never trust, always verify." No implicit trust based on network location. Being inside the corporate network doesn't grant any access. Every request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of origin.

Zero Trust Pillars:

  • Verify explicitly: Authenticate and authorize every request based on all available data points - user identity, device health, location, time, resource sensitivity.
  • Use least privilege access: Grant minimum permissions for the minimum time. Just-in-time (JIT) access - permissions are granted when needed and revoked automatically after a time window.
  • Assume breach: Design as if the attacker is already inside the network. Segment the network. Encrypt all traffic (even internal). Monitor everything. Minimize blast radius of any single compromise.
  • Interview-specific:
    • Traditional security model: hard shell (strong perimeter), soft interior (once inside the network, everything is trusted). This fails when: an attacker breaches the perimeter (phishing, VPN compromise, supply chain attack), an insider is malicious, or cloud environments have no clear perimeter.
    • Zero trust model: no perimeter concept. Every access is verified. An employee on the corporate network gets the same authentication/authorization challenge as someone on public WiFi.
    • Implementation components: identity-aware proxy (Google BeyondCorp, Zscaler), device trust (is this device managed, encrypted, up-to-date?), micro-segmentation (network policies per service), continuous verification (session re-validation, step-up authentication for sensitive operations), comprehensive logging and analytics.
    • "How would you implement zero trust for microservices?" Answer: service mesh with mTLS for all service-to-service communication. Every service has a cryptographic identity (certificate). Every connection is authenticated (mTLS) and authorized (authorization policies per service pair). All traffic is encrypted. Network policies restrict which services can communicate. Comprehensive audit logging of all access.

DNS Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • DNSSEC: authenticates DNS responses, preventing DNS spoofing/cache poisoning attacks.
    • DNS over HTTPS (DoH) / DNS over TLS (DoT): encrypts DNS queries, preventing eavesdropping and manipulation.
    • DNS as an attack vector: DNS hijacking (redirecting domains to attacker-controlled IPs), DNS tunneling (exfiltrating data through DNS queries), domain squatting (registering similar domains for phishing).
  1. Application Security

OWASP Top 10 (Know These Cold)

  1. Broken Access Control (A01:2021):

Users can act outside their intended permissions. Access controls are missing, bypassed, or improperly implemented.

  • Interview-specific:
    • IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference): a user changes /api/orders/123 to /api/orders/456 and accesses another user's order. The API doesn't verify that the authenticated user owns order 456.
    • Forced browsing: accessing /admin/dashboard without being an admin. The URL isn't linked in the UI, but the server doesn't check authorization.
    • Fix: implement authorization checks on every request at the server side. Never rely on hiding UI elements as a security control. Verify resource ownership. Use deny-by-default (require explicit permission grants, not explicit denials).
    • Automated testing: write integration tests that verify "user A cannot access user B's resources." Include authorization test cases for every API endpoint.
  1. Cryptographic Failures (A02:2021):

Data exposure due to weak or missing cryptography. Storing passwords in plaintext, using weak hashing algorithms (MD5, SHA-1 for passwords), transmitting sensitive data without TLS, hardcoded encryption keys.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Password storage: NEVER store plaintext. NEVER use MD5 or SHA-1 for passwords. Use bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 (the current recommendation). These are deliberately slow - making brute-force attacks infeasible. Include a unique salt per password.
    • Sensitive data in transit: TLS everywhere. No exceptions. HTTP → HTTPS redirect for all endpoints.
    • Sensitive data at rest: encrypt at the storage level (database encryption, S3 server-side encryption). Use strong, modern algorithms (AES-256-GCM). Manage keys properly (KMS, Vault - never hardcoded, never in source code).
    • Key rotation: encryption keys should be rotated periodically (annually at minimum). Plan for re-encryption of data when keys rotate.
  1. Injection (A03:2021):

Untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query. The attacker's data changes the intended logic.

  • Interview-specific:
    • SQL Injection: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ' + userInput + ' - if userInput is ' OR 1=1 --, the query returns all users. Fix: use parameterized queries / prepared statements (the database treats user input as data, never as SQL). ORMs do this by default. NEVER concatenate user input into SQL strings.
    • NoSQL Injection: MongoDB queries constructed from user input without validation. {"username": userInput} - if userInput is {"$gt": ""}, it matches all documents.
    • Command Injection: Runtime.exec("ping " + userInput) - if userInput is ; rm -rf /, the attacker executes arbitrary commands.
    • LDAP Injection, XPath Injection, Expression Language Injection - same principle, different interpreters.
    • Prevention: parameterized queries for SQL, input validation (whitelist acceptable values/patterns), output encoding, avoid passing user input to interpreters.
  1. Insecure Design (A04:2021):

Security flaws rooted in the design itself, not just implementation bugs. Missing threat modeling, insecure business logic, absence of security requirements.

  • Interview-specific: This category was new in the 2021 Top 10. It recognizes that you can implement secure code perfectly but still have an insecure system if the design is flawed. Example: a password reset flow that answers security questions without rate limiting - an attacker can brute-force the answers. The code is bug-free; the design is insecure. Fix: threat model during design, establish secure design patterns, and include security requirements alongside functional requirements.
  1. Security Misconfiguration (A05:2021):

Default credentials left unchanged, unnecessary features enabled, overly permissive permissions, missing security headers, verbose error messages in production.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Default credentials on databases, admin panels, and infrastructure. Change on first deployment.
    • Debug/trace mode enabled in production, exposing stack traces and system information to attackers.
    • Cloud misconfigurations: S3 buckets left public, security groups allowing 0.0.0.0/0 on all ports, IAM roles with *:* permissions.
    • Missing HTTP security headers: Content-Security-Policy, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Frame-Options.
    • Fix: automated security configuration scanning (AWS Config, Checkov, tfsec for Terraform), hardened base images, security headers middleware, disable unnecessary features and endpoints.
  1. Vulnerable and Outdated Components (A06:2021):

Using libraries, frameworks, or OS packages with known vulnerabilities. Not updating dependencies regularly.

  • Interview-specific:
    • The Log4Shell vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228) in Log4j affected millions of systems. It was a critical remote code execution in a ubiquitous logging library.
    • Prevention: Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools in CI/CD - Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. Scan every build. Fail the build on critical vulnerabilities. Monitor for new vulnerabilities continuously (not just at build time - new CVEs are published daily against existing dependencies).
    • Maintain a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM): a list of all dependencies, their versions, and their transitive dependencies. When a new vulnerability is announced, you can instantly determine if you're affected.
    • Patching cadence: critical vulnerabilities within 24-48 hours. High within a week. Medium within a month. Establish this as organizational policy.
  1. Identification and Authentication Failures (A07:2021):

Weak authentication mechanisms. Credential stuffing, brute force, session hijacking, missing MFA.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Credential stuffing: attackers use breached username/password pairs from other sites. If your users reuse passwords, their accounts are compromised. Mitigation: MFA, breached password detection (check passwords against known breach databases - HaveIBeenPwned API), rate limiting on login attempts.
    • Brute force: automated attempts to guess passwords. Mitigation: account lockout after N failed attempts (but be careful - this can be used to lock out legitimate users as a DoS vector), CAPTCHA after failed attempts, exponential backoff on login attempts.
    • Session fixation: attacker sets a session ID before the user authenticates. Mitigation: regenerate session ID upon authentication.
  1. Software and Data Integrity Failures (A08:2021):

Code and infrastructure that doesn't verify integrity. Using untrusted CDN scripts without integrity checks, insecure CI/CD pipelines, auto-updates without verification.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Subresource Integrity (SRI): <script src="cdn-url" integrity="sha384-hash"> - the browser verifies the script hash before executing. Prevents CDN compromise from injecting malicious code.
    • CI/CD pipeline security: the pipeline has access to production credentials, deployment tools, and artifact registries. A compromised pipeline can deploy malicious code to production. Protect: restrict pipeline access, require code review before merge, sign artifacts, verify signatures before deployment.
    • Supply chain attacks: SolarWinds (compromised build system injected malware into updates), dependency confusion (publishing malicious packages with internal package names to public registries), typosquatting (registering package names similar to popular packages).
  1. Security Logging and Monitoring Failures (A09:2021):

Insufficient logging of security events. No monitoring for suspicious activity. Breaches go undetected for weeks or months.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Average time to detect a breach: ~200 days (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report). Effective logging and monitoring dramatically reduces this.
    • What to log (security-relevant events): all authentication events (success AND failure), all authorization failures, all access to sensitive data, all administrative actions, all input validation failures, all exceptions in security-critical code paths.
    • What NOT to log: passwords, tokens, session IDs (in plaintext), credit card numbers, PII without masking.
    • SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): centralized platform that collects security logs from all systems, correlates events, detects patterns, and alerts on suspicious activity. Splunk, Elastic SIEM, Azure Sentinel, AWS Security Hub, Sumo Logic.
    • Security alerting: failed login burst from same IP (credential stuffing), login from unusual geolocation, privilege escalation attempts, access to sensitive resources outside business hours, large data export/download.
  1. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) (A10:2021):

The application makes HTTP requests to a URL controlled by the attacker. The attacker tricks the server into accessing internal resources.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Example: an application has a "fetch URL preview" feature. The attacker submits http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (the cloud instance metadata endpoint). The server fetches it and returns IAM credentials, private IPs, and other sensitive data.
    • The AWS instance metadata service is the most exploited SSRF target. IMDSv2 (requires a token obtained via PUT request - not achievable through simple SSRF) mitigates this.
    • Prevention: validate and sanitize all URLs. Whitelist allowed domains. Block access to internal IP ranges (10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x, 192.168.x.x, 169.254.x.x). Use IMDSv2 on all cloud instances. Don't expose raw URL fetch functionality to users.
  1. Data Security and Encryption

Encryption at Rest

What it is: Data stored on disk, in databases, in object storage, on backup media is encrypted. An attacker who gains physical access to the storage (stolen disk, compromised storage account) cannot read the data without the encryption key.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Transparent encryption (server-side): The storage system encrypts/decrypts automatically. The application doesn't need to change. AWS S3 Server-Side Encryption, RDS encryption, Azure Storage Service Encryption. This is the minimum - enable it on everything.
    • Application-level encryption: The application encrypts specific sensitive fields before storing them. The database sees ciphertext, not plaintext. Even a database administrator cannot read the encrypted fields. Use for: PII, financial data, health records, or any data where database-level access shouldn't reveal the plaintext.
    • Client-side encryption: Data is encrypted before it's sent to the cloud. The cloud provider never sees plaintext. Maximum security but prevents server-side processing (can't search, index, or query encrypted data without decrypting).
    • Encryption algorithms: AES-256-GCM (symmetric, authenticated - the standard for data at rest). Never use DES, 3DES, RC4, or AES-ECB (Electronic Codebook mode leaks patterns).

Encryption in Transit

What it is: Data moving across the network is encrypted. TLS for HTTP (HTTPS), TLS for database connections, TLS for message broker connections, mTLS for service-to-service.

  • Interview-specific:
    • TLS 1.2 is the minimum acceptable version. TLS 1.3 is preferred (faster handshake, forward secrecy by default, removed vulnerable cipher suites).
    • Certificate management: automate issuance and renewal (AWS ACM, Let's Encrypt, cert-manager in Kubernetes). Monitor for certificate expiry. An expired certificate = an outage.
    • Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS): even if the server's private key is compromised in the future, past recorded traffic cannot be decrypted. TLS 1.3 mandates PFS. TLS 1.2 supports it with ECDHE cipher suites.
    • Internal traffic: many organizations encrypt external traffic (client to load balancer) but leave internal traffic (load balancer to application, service to service) unencrypted. In zero-trust architecture, ALL traffic is encrypted - internal and external. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) handles this transparently with mTLS.

Key Management

  • Interview-specific:
    • Never hardcode keys. Not in code, not in configuration files, not in environment variables baked into images, not in Git. Ever. The most common cause of key compromise is keys committed to source control.
    • Key Management Service (KMS): AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, GCP Cloud KMS, HashiCorp Vault. Keys are stored in hardware security modules (HSMs). Applications request encryption/decryption operations - the key never leaves the KMS.
    • Envelope encryption: Encrypt data with a Data Encryption Key (DEK). Encrypt the DEK with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) stored in KMS. Store the encrypted DEK alongside the encrypted data. To decrypt: send the encrypted DEK to KMS → KMS decrypts with KEK → use the DEK to decrypt the data. This avoids sending large amounts of data to KMS (only the small DEK is sent).
    • Key rotation: Rotate keys periodically (annually at minimum, more frequently for sensitive data). With envelope encryption, rotating the KEK only requires re-encrypting the DEKs, not all the data. Plan for both routine rotation and emergency rotation (if a key is potentially compromised).
    • Key hierarchy: Root key (stored in HSM, never exported) → Master keys (per service or per environment) → Data encryption keys (per record or per file). Compromise of a data encryption key affects only the data encrypted with that key, not all data in the system.

Data Masking and Tokenization

Data Masking: Replace sensitive data with fictitious but realistic data. A credit card number becomes ****-****-****-1234. An email becomes a***@example.com. A name becomes a random name.

Types: static masking (create a masked copy of a database for non-production use), dynamic masking (mask data in real-time based on the requester's permissions - an admin sees the full SSN, a support agent sees ***-**-1234).

Tokenization: Replace sensitive data with a non-sensitive token. The mapping between token and real data is stored in a secure token vault. The token has no mathematical relationship to the original data (unlike encryption, which can be reversed with a key).

  • Interview-specific:
    • PCI-DSS compliance: most organizations tokenize credit card numbers. The card number is sent to a tokenization service (often the payment processor - Stripe, Adyen). A token is returned. The application stores only the token. The real card number is never in the application's database.
    • Dynamic masking is how you implement field-level authorization for sensitive data. A customer support agent querying a user profile sees masked SSN and masked credit card number. A fraud analyst querying the same profile sees unmasked data. Same query, different visibility based on role.

Data Classification

  • Interview-specific:
    • Every organization should classify data by sensitivity: Public (marketing content, public docs), Internal (employee directory, internal wikis), Confidential (customer data, financial data, business strategy), Restricted (credit card numbers, health records, authentication credentials, encryption keys).
    • Classification drives security controls: Restricted data requires encryption at rest AND in transit, access logging, MFA for access, strict retention policies, and annual access reviews. Public data requires none of these.
    • Automated classification: scan databases and file stores for patterns matching PII (email patterns, phone numbers, SSN patterns, credit card numbers). Tools: AWS Macie, Azure Purview, Google DLP API.
  1. API Security

API Threat Landscape

  • Interview-specific: APIs are the #1 attack surface for modern applications. OWASP has a dedicated API Security Top 10:

Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR): The most common API vulnerability. GET /api/users/123/bank-accounts - changing 123 to 456 returns another user's bank accounts. The API doesn't verify that the authenticated user owns the requested resource.

Fix: on every request, verify that the authenticated user has access to the specific resource being requested. Not just "is this user authenticated?" but "is this user authorized to access THIS specific resource?"

Broken Authentication: Weak token generation, missing token validation, credentials transmitted over HTTP, no rate limiting on authentication endpoints.

Broken Object Property Level Authorization: API returns more data than the client needs. Internal fields (isAdmin, internalNotes, costPrice) are included in the response. An attacker reads or modifies properties they shouldn't have access to.

Fix: explicitly define response schemas. Return ONLY the fields the client is authorized to see. Use different response DTOs for different authorization levels.

Unrestricted Resource Consumption: No rate limiting, no pagination limits, no maximum payload size. An attacker can exhaust server resources with expensive queries, massive uploads, or rapid-fire requests.

Fix: rate limiting per client/API key, pagination with maximum page size, request payload size limits, query complexity limits (for GraphQL), timeout on expensive operations.

Broken Function Level Authorization: A regular user can access admin endpoints. POST /api/admin/users/delete is accessible to anyone with a valid token because the endpoint doesn't check the user's role.

Fix: authorization middleware on EVERY endpoint. Deny by default. Define which roles can access which endpoints. Test that unauthorized users receive 403.

API Security Best Practices

  • Interview-specific:
    • Authentication for every endpoint. No anonymous endpoints except public content, health checks, and the login/register flow itself.
    • HTTPS only. Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS. Use HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) headers to prevent downgrade attacks.
    • Rate limiting. Per-client, per-endpoint. Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. Implement at the API gateway level.
    • Input validation. Validate every input field against expected type, format, length, and range. Use a schema validation library. Reject anything that doesn't conform. Validate on the server - client-side validation is a UX feature, not a security control.
    • Output encoding. Encode output to prevent XSS when API responses are rendered in browsers.
    • API versioning. Version your API. Deprecate old versions with adequate notice. Don't leave old, potentially vulnerable API versions running indefinitely.
    • API keys vs OAuth tokens. API keys identify the calling application (for rate limiting, billing). OAuth tokens identify and authorize the user. API keys alone are insufficient for user-level authorization.
    • Logging and monitoring. Log every API request (method, path, authenticated user, response code, latency). Monitor for: unusual traffic patterns, spike in 401/403 responses, excessive 500 responses, traffic from blocked IPs.
    • CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing). Configure which domains can call your API from a browser. NEVER use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for APIs that handle authenticated requests.

GraphQL Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Query complexity limiting: GraphQL allows clients to request deeply nested data. A malicious query can request 10 levels of nesting, causing the server to join and fetch enormous amounts of data. Set a maximum query depth and complexity score. Reject queries exceeding the limit.
    • Introspection in production: GraphQL introspection allows anyone to discover your entire schema. Disable introspection in production (or restrict to authenticated admin users).
    • Batching attacks: GraphQL allows multiple queries in a single request. An attacker can batch thousands of login attempts in one HTTP request, bypassing per-request rate limiting. Rate limit by operation count, not just request count.
    • Authorization per field: Different fields in the same type may have different access levels. A User type might have email (public), phoneNumber (self and admin), and salary (admin only). Implement field-level authorization resolvers.
  1. Infrastructure Security

Container Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Minimal base images: Use distroless or Alpine-based images. Fewer packages = fewer vulnerabilities = smaller attack surface. A full Ubuntu image has hundreds of packages you don't need, each a potential vulnerability.
    • Don't run as root: Configure containers to run as a non-root user. If an attacker exploits the application, they have non-root access inside the container - limiting what they can do. In Kubernetes: securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true.
    • Read-only filesystem: Make the container filesystem read-only (readOnlyRootFilesystem: true). If an attacker gains access, they can't write malicious files. Use ephemeral volumes (emptyDir) for any necessary temporary storage.
    • No privileged containers: privileged: true gives the container full access to the host. NEVER use in production unless absolutely necessary (and audit heavily if so).
    • Image scanning: Scan images for vulnerabilities in the CI/CD pipeline (Trivy, Snyk Container, ECR scanning). Block deployment of images with critical vulnerabilities.
    • Image signing and verification: Sign images after building. Verify signatures before deploying. Prevents running tampered images. Tools: Cosign (from Sigstore), Notary.
    • Private registry: Store images in a private container registry (ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor). Don't pull from public registries in production without vetting.
    • No secrets in images: Never bake credentials, API keys, or certificates into container images. Inject at runtime from secrets management.

Kubernetes Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • RBAC (Kubernetes RBAC): Define who can do what in the cluster. Roles (namespace-scoped) and ClusterRoles (cluster-wide) grant permissions. RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings assign roles to users or service accounts. Principle: minimum necessary permissions. Don't give developers cluster-admin in production.
    • Network Policies: Default Kubernetes networking allows all pods to communicate with all other pods. Network Policies restrict this: "only pods with label app=frontend can communicate with pods with label app=api on port 8080." Requires a CNI plugin that supports Network Policies (Calico, Cilium).
    • Pod Security Standards (PSS): Define security profiles for pods. Privileged (unrestricted - only for system pods), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalations), Restricted (hardened - no root, read-only filesystem, no host networking). Enforced via Pod Security Admission controller.
    • Service accounts: Every pod runs with a service account. Don't use the default service account (it may have more permissions than needed). Create dedicated service accounts per workload with least-privilege RBAC.
    • Secrets management in K8s: Native Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default. They're stored in etcd in plaintext (unless etcd encryption is configured). For production: use External Secrets Operator (syncs from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager), Sealed Secrets (encrypted in Git, decrypted in cluster), or mount secrets directly from Vault via sidecar.
    • etcd security: etcd holds all cluster state including secrets. Encrypt etcd at rest. Restrict network access to etcd. Enable authentication.
    • Admission controllers: Intercept API requests before they're persisted. OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno can enforce policies: "all containers must have resource limits," "no container can use the 'latest' tag," "all pods must have a network policy."

Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)

  • Interview-specific:
    • The most common security issues in cloud environments are misconfigurations, not sophisticated attacks. Public S3 buckets, overly permissive IAM roles, unencrypted databases, open security groups.
    • CSPM tools continuously scan cloud environments for misconfigurations: AWS Security Hub, Prisma Cloud, Prowler (open source for AWS), Checkov (IaC scanning), ScoutSuite.
    • IaC scanning: scan Terraform, CloudFormation, and Kubernetes manifests for security issues BEFORE deployment. Checkov, tfsec, Trivy (IaC mode). Shift left - catch misconfigurations in the pull request, not in production.
    • Cloud trail/audit logging: enable CloudTrail (AWS), Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs. Log every API call to the cloud provider. Who created what resource, when, from where? Essential for incident investigation and compliance.

Supply Chain Security

  • Interview-specific:
    • Software Bill of Materials (SBOM): A complete inventory of all components in your software - direct dependencies, transitive dependencies, their versions. When a vulnerability is announced, an SBOM tells you instantly whether you're affected.
    • Dependency scanning: Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities in every CI build. Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. Fail the build on critical findings.
    • Dependency pinning: Pin exact dependency versions in lock files (package-lock.json, Pipfile.lock, go.sum). Prevents supply chain attacks where a compromised dependency publishes a malicious update.
    • Artifact signing: Sign build artifacts (container images, packages) with a private key. Verify the signature before deployment. Ensures the artifact wasn't tampered with between build and deployment. Cosign + Sigstore for container images.
    • Build provenance: Record how an artifact was built - which source code, which build system, which builder identity. SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) is a framework for supply chain security. Level 1 (build process exists) → Level 4 (hermetic, reproducible, non-forgeable build with two-party review).
    • Private package registries: Host internal packages in a private registry. Configure package managers to check the private registry first. Prevents dependency confusion attacks (attacker publishes a malicious package with the same name as your internal package to a public registry).
  1. Security in CI/CD Pipelines

Pipeline Security Risks

  • Interview-specific: The CI/CD pipeline is one of the most privileged components in your infrastructure. It has access to: source code, production credentials, deployment tools, artifact registries, and potentially production environments. A compromised pipeline can deploy arbitrary code to production.

DevSecOps Practices

Shift Left: Integrate security testing as early as possible in the development lifecycle. Don't wait for a pre-production security review. Catch vulnerabilities in the developer's IDE, in the pull request, in the CI build.

  • Interview-specific:
    • Pre-commit hooks: Run linters and basic security checks before code is committed. Detect hardcoded secrets (git-secrets, detect-secrets, truffleHog), check for known vulnerable code patterns.
    • SAST (Static Application Security Testing): Analyze source code for vulnerabilities without executing it. Finds: SQL injection, XSS, insecure deserialization, hardcoded credentials, buffer overflows. Tools: SonarQube, Semgrep, CodeQL (GitHub), Checkmarx. Run in CI on every pull request.
    • SCA (Software Composition Analysis): Scan dependencies for known vulnerabilities. Tools: Snyk, Dependabot, OWASP Dependency-Check. Run in CI on every build. Fail on critical vulnerabilities.
    • DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): Test the running application by sending malicious requests. Finds: injection vulnerabilities, authentication flaws, misconfiguration. Tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite. Run against staging environment.
    • IaC Scanning: Scan infrastructure code for security misconfigurations. Tools: Checkov, tfsec, Trivy. Run in CI on every Terraform/CloudFormation change. Catch "S3 bucket without encryption" before it reaches production.
    • Container Image Scanning: Scan built images for OS and application vulnerabilities. Tools: Trivy, Snyk Container, Grype. Run after building, before pushing to registry. Block images with critical vulnerabilities.
    • Secret Scanning: Detect secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) in source code and commit history. Tools: GitGuardian, GitHub Secret Scanning, truffleHog. Run on every commit. Alert immediately on detection.

Pipeline hardening:

  • Interview-specific:
    • Minimal pipeline permissions: The pipeline should have only the permissions it needs. Separate pipelines for building (needs code read, artifact write) and deploying (needs deployment permissions). Don't give the build pipeline production deployment access.
    • Ephemeral build environments: Each build runs in a fresh, disposable environment. No state carries over between builds. Prevents an attacker from persisting in the build environment.
    • Immutable artifacts: Once an artifact (container image, package) is built and signed, it's never modified. The same artifact goes through testing, staging, and production. No rebuilding between environments - what you tested is exactly what you deploy.
    • Audit trail: Log every pipeline execution, every deployment, every configuration change. Who triggered the build, what code was included, what tests ran, what was deployed where. This audit trail is essential for incident investigation and compliance.
    • Separation of duties: Developers write code. The pipeline builds and tests. A different pipeline (or approval gate) deploys to production. No single person can push code directly to production without review.
    • Branch protection: Require code review (minimum 2 approvers for sensitive repositories), require CI passing (all security scans green), require signed commits. No direct pushes to main/production branches.
  1. Incident Response and Security Operations

Incident Response Framework

  • Interview-specific: When asked "what do you do when a security breach is detected?" having a structured framework demonstrates maturity.

Phase 1 - Preparation (Before any incident):

  • Incident response plan: documented, tested, rehearsed. Who does what, communication channels, escalation paths.
  • Incident response team: defined roles - incident commander (coordinates), technical lead (investigates and remediates), communications lead (internal and external), legal (regulatory obligations).
  • Playbooks: pre-written procedures for common incident types. "Compromised credentials playbook," "Data breach playbook," "Ransomware playbook," "DDoS playbook." Each playbook has specific steps, not generic guidance.
  • Tools and access: ensure the IR team has access to logs, can isolate systems, can revoke credentials, can communicate out-of-band (if the primary communication system is compromised).
  • Tabletop exercises: simulate incidents without actual system changes. "The payment database was breached at 2 AM. Walk through what you would do." Run quarterly.

Phase 2 - Detection and Analysis:

  • How was the incident detected? SIEM alert, anomaly detection, customer report, third-party notification, threat intelligence.
  • Scope assessment: what systems are affected? What data may be compromised? How many users are impacted? Is the attacker still active?
  • Evidence preservation: capture logs, memory dumps, disk images BEFORE making changes. Evidence may be needed for legal proceedings.
  • Classification: severity (P1-P4), type (data breach, unauthorized access, malware, DDoS).

Phase 3 - Containment:

  • Short-term containment: stop the bleeding. Isolate compromised systems (remove from network), revoke compromised credentials, block attacker IP addresses, disable compromised accounts.
  • Long-term containment: implement temporary fixes that allow business operations to continue while you work on eradication. Stand up clean replacement systems if needed.

Phase 4 - Eradication:

  • Remove the root cause. Patch the vulnerability. Remove malware. Close the entry point. If credentials were stolen, rotate ALL potentially affected credentials (not just confirmed ones).

Phase 5 - Recovery:

  • Restore systems from clean backups if needed. Bring systems back online gradually. Monitor closely for signs of continued compromise.
  • Verify: is the vulnerability actually fixed? Is the attacker actually out? Sometimes attackers establish persistence mechanisms (backdoor accounts, scheduled tasks) that survive initial remediation.

Phase 6 - Post-Incident Review (Blameless):

  • What happened? Timeline of events.
  • How was it detected? Could we have detected it faster?
  • What was the root cause? Was it a code bug, a misconfiguration, a process failure, a social engineering attack?
  • What mitigations prevent recurrence?
  • What did we learn? Update playbooks, improve detection, fix process gaps.
  • The review must be blameless - focus on systemic failures, not individual mistakes. If someone made an error, ask "why did the system allow that error to have this impact?" not "who did this wrong?"

Security Monitoring

  • Interview-specific:
    • SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): Collects logs from all systems (application logs, authentication logs, network logs, cloud API logs, endpoint logs). Correlates events across sources. Detects patterns (same IP failing authentication across multiple services, unusual data access patterns, privilege escalation sequences). Alerts on suspicious activity.
    • SOC (Security Operations Center): A team that monitors security alerts 24/7. Triages alerts, investigates incidents, escalates as needed. Can be in-house or outsourced (MSSP - Managed Security Service Provider).
    • Detection engineering: Writing detection rules based on known attack techniques (MITRE ATT&CK framework). "Detect when a service account makes API calls it has never made before." "Detect when data download volume exceeds 10x the 30-day average." These rules are developed, tested, and maintained like code.
    • Threat intelligence: Information about known threat actors, their techniques, their infrastructure (IP addresses, domains, malware hashes). Feed threat intelligence into your SIEM to detect known indicators of compromise.

Breach Notification Requirements

  • Interview-specific:
    • GDPR: notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach. Notify affected individuals "without undue delay" if the breach is likely to result in high risk to their rights.
    • HIPAA: notify affected individuals within 60 days. Notify HHS (Department of Health and Human Services). If breach affects 500+ individuals, notify media.
