  • Open for extension without modification (connects to OCP)

2.7 Pure Fabrication

If no natural domain class fits a responsibility, invent a service class.

  • OrderService, EmailSender, ReportGenerator — these don't exist in the domain model but are needed
  • Maintains high cohesion by not stuffing behavior into domain objects

2.8 Indirection

Introduce an intermediate object to mediate between others.

  • Reduces direct coupling
  • Examples: Adapters, Controllers, Factories

2.9 Protected Variations

Identify points of variation and create a stable interface around them.

  • Encapsulate what varies (GoF principle)
  • If something is likely to change, hide it behind an abstraction

3. Foundational Heuristics

DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself

Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous representation in a system.

  • Not just about avoiding copy-paste code
  • About avoiding duplicated knowledge — the same business rule in two places
  • Fix: extract to shared method, class, or module

Warning: Premature DRY via wrong abstraction can cause worse problems than duplication.

KISS — Keep It Simple, Stupid

The simplest solution that correctly solves the problem is best.

  • Resist over-engineering and complexity for its own sake
  • Complexity must be justified by actual requirements
  • Simple code is readable, testable, and debuggable

YAGNI — You Aren't Gonna Need It

Don't implement features until they are needed.

  • Speculative code adds maintenance burden without value
  • Design for today's requirements with extensibility in mind, but don't code tomorrow's features
  • Complements KISS

Composition Over Inheritance

Favor object composition over class inheritance.

  • Inheritance is a strong coupling mechanism (fragile base class problem)
  • Composition is more flexible: behaviors are combined at runtime, not compile time
  • Inheritance should model true "is-a" relationships, not reuse
// Inheritance — rigid
class Duck extends FlyingBird, SwimmingBird { ... }  // multiple inheritance problem

// Composition — flexible
class Duck {
    private FlyBehavior flyBehavior;
    private SwimBehavior swimBehavior;
    // behaviors can be swapped at runtime
}

Law of Demeter (LoD) / Principle of Least Knowledge

A method should only call methods on:

  • Itself
  • Its parameters
  • Objects it creates
  • Its direct component objects

Not on: objects returned by calling methods on other objects (train wrecks).

Encapsulate What Varies

Identify aspects of your application that vary and separate them from what stays the same.

  • The core of the Strategy, Template Method, and Observer patterns
  • Protected Variations in GRASP

4. Design Principle Relationships

SOLID
  S → High Cohesion (GRASP), SRP
  O → Protected Variations (GRASP), Encapsulate What Varies
  L → Design by Contract, Polymorphism (GRASP)
  I → Low Coupling (GRASP)
  D → Dependency Injection, Indirection (GRASP)

Foundational
  DRY → Information Expert (GRASP)
  KISS + YAGNI → Anti-over-engineering
  Composition over Inheritance → Low Coupling
  Law of Demeter → Low Coupling

5. Common Design Smells (Principle Violations)

Smell Principle Violated Fix
God Class SRP Split responsibilities
Conditional type-checking (instanceof) OCP, Polymorphism Polymorphic dispatch
Subclass that breaks parent contract LSP Refactor hierarchy
Fat interface ISP Segregate interfaces
Hard-coded dependencies DIP Dependency injection
Duplicated business logic DRY Extract to shared location
Over-engineered abstractions KISS, YAGNI Simplify
Train wreck (a.getB().getC().doX()) Law of Demeter Delegate
Deep inheritance chains Composition over Inheritance Favor composition

Interview Q&A

Q1: Explain SOLID in plain English. S: One class, one job. O: Add new behavior by extending, not editing existing code. L: Subclasses work wherever the parent works. I: Interfaces should be focused — no forced empty implementations. D: Depend on abstractions, not concretions; inject dependencies.

Q2: What is the difference between SRP and cohesion? SRP is a design guideline (one reason to change). Cohesion is the measure of how related the responsibilities within a class are. High cohesion often means SRP is satisfied. SRP is the principle; cohesion is the metric.

Q3: Give a real-world LSP violation. Square extends Rectangle. Rectangle's contract: setting width doesn't affect height. Square violates this: it must keep width == height. Code using Rectangle breaks when a Square is passed in.

Q4: What's the difference between OCP and Open for Extension through Inheritance vs. Interfaces? Both are valid, but interfaces are preferred. Inheritance for extension risks the fragile base class problem. Interface-based extension (Strategy, Decorator) is more flexible.

Q5: How does DIP relate to dependency injection? DIP says depend on abstractions. Dependency Injection is the mechanism to honor DIP: instead of a class creating its dependencies (concretions), they are injected (usually as interfaces). DI frameworks (Spring) automate this.

Q6: What is the difference between ISP and SRP? SRP is about classes having one responsibility (one reason to change). ISP is about interfaces being focused so clients don't depend on methods they don't use. SRP → classes; ISP → interfaces.

Q7: When does DRY cause problems? When you apply DRY to code that is coincidentally similar but represents different knowledge. Two methods that look the same but have different reasons to change should NOT be merged — DRY at the wrong level creates wrong coupling.

Q8: What is the Law of Demeter and why does violating it matter? A method should not reach through objects. Violations create tight coupling to internal structures — changing an object's internals breaks all code that chains through it. It reduces ripple-effect from change.

Q9: What is GRASP and how does it differ from SOLID? GRASP is a set of patterns for assigning responsibilities to OO objects (Information Expert, Creator, Controller, etc.). SOLID is a set of design principles for class and module design. GRASP is more prescriptive about "who does what"; SOLID is about structural quality.

Q10: When should you use composition vs. inheritance? Use inheritance only for true "is-a" relationships that are stable and don't vary at runtime. Use composition when behavior varies, when you want to mix multiple behaviors, or when the relationship is "has-a". The Strategy pattern is composition of behavior.

Q11: What does "encapsulate what varies" mean? Identify the part of your design that changes frequently and hide it behind an interface. The stable parts code to the interface; the variant parts implement it. This is the root principle behind many GoF patterns.

Q12: How does ISP prevent the "empty implementation" problem? When an interface has many methods, implementing classes are forced to provide implementations for methods irrelevant to them (often as empty stubs or throw UnsupportedOperationException). ISP prescribes splitting fat interfaces into focused ones so each implementor only provides what makes sense.

Interview Strategy

  1. SOLID is almost guaranteed — be ready to explain all 5 with an example each.
  2. Always give examples: "OCP means... for instance, if I have a payment processor with if (paymentType == VISA)..., adding PayPal requires modifying the class. Instead..."
  3. Connect principles to design patterns: OCP → Strategy, Template Method; DIP → Factory, DI; LSP → correct inheritance hierarchies.
  4. Know the limits: "SOLID principles are guidelines, not laws. In a small script, SRP can be over-applied. The goal is appropriate design for the scale."
  5. GRASP is less commonly tested but impresses senior interviewers — knowing Information Expert and Controller shows depth.

Common Pitfalls

  • Memorizing definitions without being able to explain with examples
  • Confusing SRP with "one method per class" (too extreme)
  • Thinking OCP means never modifying code (you still fix bugs; OCP is about adding new behavior)
  • Applying DIP without DI framework knowledge ("how do I actually inject dependencies?")
  • Forgetting that principles can conflict: sometimes applying DRY aggressively violates SRP

Cheat Sheet

Principle Keyword Test
SRP One reason to change Would >1 stakeholder cause this to change?
OCP Open/closed Can I add features without editing existing code?
LSP Substitutability Does the subclass break the parent's contract?
ISP Focused interfaces Are any implementors leaving methods empty?
DIP Depend on abstractions Is high-level code tied to new ConcreteClass()?
DRY Single source of truth Is the same rule expressed in two places?
KISS Simplest solution Is this complexity necessary?
YAGNI Build only what's needed Am I coding for hypothetical futures?
LoD One-dot rule Am I chaining through objects?
CoI Composition first Is inheritance the best fit here?

Design Principles in Java

Design Principles in Java

Design principles in Java extend general OO design principles with Java-specific language features, idioms, and API design conventions. This includes effective use of interfaces, generics, immutability, the Java Collections API design, and principles from Effective Java by Joshua Bloch — the definitive reference for Java-specific design quality.

Why It Matters in Interviews: Senior Java engineers are expected to know not just what to do but how Java specifically enables or enforces good design. Questions often reference Effective Java items or ask you to evaluate a Java class design.

1. SOLID Applied to Java

SRP in Java

  • Service classes (OrderService, EmailService) should handle one domain concern
  • Avoid mixing persistence, business logic, and presentation in one class
  • Spring pattern: Controller → Service → Repository (each layer has one responsibility)
  • Use @Service, @Repository annotations to signal roles clearly

OCP in Java

  • Achieved through interfaces + implementations
  • Java's Comparable / Comparator — comparing logic is open for extension via new Comparator implementations
  • java.util.function.* functional interfaces enable behavioral extension without class modification
  • Use default methods on interfaces (Java 8+) to add new behavior without breaking existing implementors
// OCP via interface + Strategy pattern
interface DiscountStrategy {
    BigDecimal apply(BigDecimal price);
}
class SeasonalDiscount implements DiscountStrategy { ... }
class LoyaltyDiscount implements DiscountStrategy { ... }
// OrderService never changes when new discount type is added

LSP in Java

  • Java's type system enforces basic substitutability, but behavioral contracts require discipline
  • @Override annotation — always use it; prevents accidental method hiding instead of overriding
  • Never override methods to do less (weakened behavior) or throw new checked exceptions
  • Avoid overriding concrete methods from non-abstract classes — prefer composition

ISP in Java

  • Define role interfaces (small, focused)
  • Java's Iterable, Comparable, Closeable are perfect ISP examples
  • Functional interfaces (Runnable, Callable, Supplier, Function) are single-method role interfaces
  • Prefer many small interfaces over one large one

DIP in Java

  • Constructor injection is the preferred DI mechanism (testable, explicit)
  • Program to interfaces: List<T>, Map<K,V>, Repository<T> not ArrayList, HashMap
  • Spring Boot's IoC container automates DIP via @Autowired / constructor injection
  • Factory patterns implement DIP by hiding concrete class creation

2. Effective Java Design Items (Key Items by Bloch)

Item 1: Static Factory Methods Over Constructors

// Constructor — inflexible, always creates new instance
new BigDecimal(100);

// Static factory — descriptive name, can cache, can return subtype
BigDecimal.valueOf(100);
Optional.of(value);
List.of(a, b, c);

Benefits: meaningful names, not forced to create new object, can return subtype.

Item 2: Builder When Many Parameters

// Telescoping constructors — bad
new Pizza(12, true, false, true, false, true);

// Builder — readable, safe
Pizza pizza = new Pizza.Builder(12)
    .cheese(true)
    .pepperoni(true)
    .build();

Item 3: Singleton — Prefer Enum

// Thread-safe, reflection-safe, serialization-safe singleton
public enum AppConfig {
    INSTANCE;
    private final String dbUrl = "jdbc://...";
    public String getDbUrl() { return dbUrl; }
}

Item 15: Minimize Mutability

  • Classes should be immutable by default
  • final class + final fields + no setters + defensive copies in and out
  • String, BigDecimal, LocalDate are immutable — model your value objects similarly

Item 17: Design and Document for Inheritance or Prohibit It

  • If not designing for inheritance, make class final or constructors private
  • Every non-final method intended for override must be documented for what overriders must do
  • Most classes should be final unless explicitly designed for inheritance

Item 18: Favor Composition Over Inheritance

// Bad: inheritance for reuse
class MyList extends ArrayList<String> {
    private int addCount = 0;
    @Override public boolean add(String e) { addCount++; return super.add(e); }
    // Bug: addAll() calls add() internally → double-counting
}

// Good: composition + forwarding
class CountingList<E> {
    private int addCount = 0;
    private final List<E> list;
    public CountingList(List<E> list) { this.list = list; }
    public boolean add(E e) { addCount++; return list.add(e); }
}

Item 25: Prefer Lists to Arrays (Type Safety)

// Arrays are covariant — runtime failure
Object[] strings = new String[1];
strings[0] = 42;  // ArrayStoreException at runtime

// Generics are invariant — compile-time safety
List<Object> objects = new ArrayList<String>();  // Compile error — caught earlier

Item 43: Prefer Method References to Lambdas

// Lambda — verbose
customers.stream().map(c -> c.getName());

// Method reference — cleaner
customers.stream().map(Customer::getName);

Item 54: Return Empty Collections/Arrays, Not Null

// Bad
public List<Order> getOrders() { return null; }  // forces null check on callers

// Good
public List<Order> getOrders() {
    return orders.isEmpty() ? Collections.emptyList() : new ArrayList<>(orders);
}

Item 64: Refer to Objects by Their Interfaces

// Bad
ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
HashMap<String, Order> orderMap = new HashMap<>();

// Good
List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
Map<String, Order> orderMap = new HashMap<>();

3. Java Interface Design Principles

Default Methods (Java 8+)

  • Allow evolving interfaces without breaking existing implementations
  • Do not override Object methods in interfaces
  • Use sparingly — default methods should provide reasonable behavior, not impose policy

Sealed Interfaces (Java 17+)

  • Restrict which classes can implement an interface
  • Enables exhaustive pattern matching (with switch expressions)
    sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}

Marker Interfaces vs Annotations

  • Marker interfaces (Serializable, Cloneable): use when the parameter type must be the marker
  • Annotations: prefer for metadata that doesn't affect type
  • Effective Java: prefer annotations over marker interfaces in most cases

4. Java Generics Design Principles

Bounded Type Parameters

// Producer Extends, Consumer Super (PECS)
// Producers: use extends → can read, can't write
public void process(List<? extends Animal> animals) {
    for (Animal a : animals) { a.feed(); }  // safe to read
}

// Consumers: use super → can write, limited reads
public void addAnimals(List<? super Dog> list) {
    list.add(new Dog());  // safe to write
}

Generic Methods over Raw Types

// Raw type — unsafe, loses type info
List list = new ArrayList();
list.add("hello");
list.add(42);       // no compile error!

// Generic — type-safe
List<String> strings = new ArrayList<>();
strings.add(42);    // compile error!

5. Java Concurrency Design Principles

Prefer Immutability for Shared State

  • Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe
  • final fields, no setters, defensive copies

Minimize Synchronized Scope

  • Synchronize only what must be protected
  • Prefer java.util.concurrent utilities over manual synchronized

Safe Publication

  • Safely publishing objects through volatile, final, AtomicReference, or by synchronization
  • Objects accessed by multiple threads must be safely published

Design Concurrency-Aware Classes (Effective Java Item 78-82)

  • Synchronize access to shared mutable data
  • Avoid excessive synchronization (can cause deadlocks/livelock)
  • Prefer executors and tasks over threads directly
  • Document thread-safety contracts in class-level JavaDoc

6. Java Value Objects and Records

Value Objects (Pre-Java 14)

public final class Email {
    private final String value;
    public Email(String value) {
        if (!isValid(value)) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid email");
        this.value = value;
    }
    // equals, hashCode based on value
    // toString
    // getter only
}

Records (Java 14+)

// Compact, immutable value objects
public record Point(int x, int y) {}

// Built-in: constructor, getters (x(), y()), equals, hashCode, toString
Point p = new Point(3, 4);
p.x();  // 3

Records enforce: immutability, equals/hashCode based on components, toString.

7. Dependency Injection Patterns in Java

Constructor Injection (Preferred)

@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final OrderRepository repository;
    private final EmailService emailService;

    public OrderService(OrderRepository repository, EmailService emailService) {
        this.repository = Objects.requireNonNull(repository);
        this.emailService = Objects.requireNonNull(emailService);
    }
}

Why: Makes dependencies explicit, enables easy testing with mocks, immutable after construction.

Field Injection (Avoid)

@Autowired  // Bad — hides dependencies, not testable without Spring context
private OrderRepository repository;

Setter Injection (For Optional Dependencies)

@Autowired(required = false)
public void setEmailService(EmailService emailService) {
    this.emailService = emailService;
}

Interview Q&A

Q1: How does Java implement the Open/Closed Principle? Through interfaces and polymorphism. Define an interface for the varying behavior; new variants are added as new implementations without touching existing code. java.util.Comparator is a classic example — you add new sorting behavior without modifying sorting code.

Q2: Why does Effective Java recommend static factory methods over constructors? They have descriptive names (unlike constructors), don't need to create a new object every call (can cache), and can return subtypes. E.g., Optional.of(), List.of(), BigDecimal.valueOf().

Q3: Explain the fragile base class problem and how Java composition fixes it. When a subclass depends on implementation details of the parent, parent changes can break subclasses silently. With composition (wrapping), the inner class is a black box — changes to it don't automatically affect the wrapper.

Q4: What is the Producer Extends, Consumer Super (PECS) rule for generics? Use <? extends T> when you only read from the structure (producer). Use <? super T> when you only write to the structure (consumer). Helps design flexible generic APIs that accept a range of types.

Q5: Why should you prefer List<String> over ArrayList<String> in declarations? It programs to the abstraction (DIP/ISP), allows implementation to change without affecting code, and signals that only List behavior is needed — not ArrayList-specific behavior.

Q6: What are Java Records and when should you use them? Records (Java 14+) are compact immutable data carriers. Use for value objects, DTOs, result types. They auto-generate constructor, getters, equals/hashCode, toString. Cannot be extended; fields are implicitly final.

Q7: How does final in Java enforce design principles? final class enforces "design for inheritance or prohibit it" — prevents subclassing. final field enforces immutability. final method prevents overriding. These language features enforce LSP (subclasses can't break contracts of final members) and SRP (immutable objects have simpler invariants).

Q8: Why prefer constructor injection over field injection in Spring? Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and mandatory, supports immutability (final fields), makes the class testable without the Spring container (pass mocks directly), and shows dependencies clearly at construction time.

Q9: What does "sealed" do in Java 17+ interfaces? sealed restricts which classes can implement (or extend) the interface/class. It enables exhaustive pattern matching in switch expressions. Useful for algebraic data types (shapes, result types, events).

Q10: When would you make a class final in Java? When not designed for inheritance — most utility classes, value objects, many service classes. This enforces Bloch's "design and document for inheritance, or prohibit it." Prevents LSP violations by malicious or accidental subclassing.

Interview Strategy

  1. Reference Effective Java: Mention "Item X of Effective Java says..." — shows authority.
  2. Connect to Java versions: Show evolution — "Pre-Java 14 we used value objects; now we have records."
  3. Show Spring context: DIP → constructor injection in Spring; ISP → focused @Service interfaces.
  4. Cover generics PECS: One of the most commonly asked Java-specific design questions.
  5. Immutability is a theme: Connect to concurrency, thread safety, value objects, records.

Common Pitfalls

  • Forgetting @Override on overriding methods (can accidentally hide parent methods)
  • Using field injection (@Autowired on fields) instead of constructor injection
  • Overusing inheritance where composition is better (often caught by "fragile base class" questions)
  • Not making defensive copies of mutable objects in constructors/getters
  • Using raw types instead of generic types (type safety lost)
  • Not making classes/methods final when inheritance is not intended

Cheat Sheet

Principle Java Mechanism
SRP Single-purpose service/repository/controller
OCP Interface + multiple implementations; default methods
LSP @Override always; never narrow behavior
ISP Focused interfaces; functional interfaces
DIP Constructor injection; program to interface
Immutability final class, final fields, no setters, defensive copies
Composition Wrap objects, don't extend them
Value Objects Records (Java 14+) or manual immutable class
Generics Safety PECS; avoid raw types
Factory Static factory methods over constructors

Coding Standards in Java

Coding Standards in Java

Coding standards are agreed-upon rules for formatting, structure, and style that a team follows to produce consistent, readable code. Java has two dominant style standards: Google Java Style Guide and Oracle's Code Conventions for Java. In practice, most modern Java projects use Google's guide or a team-specific variant enforced by Checkstyle.

Why It Matters in Interviews: Standards questions test whether you can work effectively in a team environment, understand industrial Java practices, and whether your code would pass review at top engineering organizations.

1. Source File Structure (Google Java Style)

Required Order Within a File

1. License/copyright header (if applicable)
2. package statement
3. import statements (no wildcards)
4. Exactly one top-level class

Class Internal Order

1. Static constants and fields
2. Instance fields
3. Constructors
4. Static factory methods
5. Public methods
6. Private/protected methods (near their callers)
7. Inner classes and interfaces

Import Ordering (Google Style)

// Order:
// 1. Static imports (all together)
// 2. Non-static imports:
//    a. com.google.*  (or your company prefix)
//    b. Third-party
//    c. java.*
//    d. javax.*
// Each group separated by blank line
// Alphabetical within groups

import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.*;

import com.example.domain.Order;

import org.springframework.stereotype.Service;

import java.util.List;
import java.util.Map;

Never use wildcard imports (import java.util.*) — makes dependencies unclear, can cause conflicts.

2. Formatting Standards

Indentation and Braces

  • Indentation: 4 spaces (Oracle) or 2 spaces (Google) — pick one, be consistent
  • Braces: K&R style (opening brace same line — Google) or Allman (new line — some Oracle guides)
  • Always use braces, even for single-line blocks
// Google style (K&R, 2-space)
if (condition) {
  doSomething();
}

// Oracle style (4-space)
if (condition) {
    doSomething();
}

// Bad — brace-less (dangerous)
if (condition)
    doSomething();

Line Length

  • Google: 100 characters
  • Oracle: 80 characters
  • Most modern projects: 100–120 characters

Line Wrapping

When a line exceeds the limit, wrap after operators and align continuation:

// Method chaining
String result = someObject
    .method1()
    .method2()
    .method3();

// Long method calls
void longMethodCall(
    String firstParam,
    String secondParam,
    int thirdParam) { ... }

Blank Lines

  • Two blank lines between class-level declarations
  • One blank line between methods
  • One blank line to separate logical sections within a method

3. Naming Standards

Summary Table

Element Convention Example
Package all.lowercase.dots com.example.payment.service
Class / Interface / Enum UpperCamelCase PaymentProcessor
Method lowerCamelCase verb processPayment()
Local Variable lowerCamelCase noun orderTotal
Parameter lowerCamelCase noun customerId
Constant (static final) UPPER_SNAKE_CASE MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS
Enum Value UPPER_SNAKE_CASE PAYMENT_PENDING
Type Parameter Single letter or descriptive T, E, CustomerType

Method Name Prefixes by Convention

Prefix Meaning Example
get Accessor (JavaBeans) getName()
set Mutator (JavaBeans) setName(name)
is / has / can Boolean return isActive(), hasRole()
find / get Return Optional or null findById(id)
calculate / compute Derive a value calculateDiscount()
create / build Factory / builder createOrder()
validate / check Validation validateInput()
on Event handler onOrderPlaced()
handle Handler handlePaymentFailure()

4. Comments and JavaDoc Standards

JavaDoc Rules

  • All public types must have class-level JavaDoc
  • All public/protected methods must have method-level JavaDoc
  • Parameters, return values, and exceptions must be documented
  • Use {@link} for cross-references
  • Use {@code} for inline code
/**
 * Processes a payment for the given order.
 *
 * <p>This method validates the payment details before processing.
 * If validation fails, the order remains in {@code PENDING} status.
 *
 * @param order the order to process payment for; must not be null
 * @param paymentDetails the payment details; must not be null
 * @return the payment receipt; never null
 * @throws PaymentException if the payment gateway rejects the transaction
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if order or paymentDetails is null
 * @see PaymentGateway#charge(PaymentDetails)
 */
public Receipt processPayment(Order order, PaymentDetails paymentDetails) { ... }

Inline Comment Rules

// TODO: owner/ticket - short description of what needs to be done
// FIXME: Urgent bug - short description
// NOTE: Why this non-obvious decision was made

// Bad — restates code
int total = 0; // initialize total to zero

// Good — explains why
// Skipping first element; it's always the header row
for (int i = 1; i < rows.size(); i++) { ... }

5. Code Organization Standards

Package Organization (By Feature, Not Layer)

// By Layer (older style — avoid)
com.example.controller
com.example.service
com.example.repository

// By Feature (preferred — Clean Architecture)
com.example.order.OrderController
com.example.order.OrderService
com.example.order.OrderRepository
com.example.payment.PaymentController
com.example.payment.PaymentService

Class Size Standards

Metric Recommended Warning
Lines per class < 200 > 500
Methods per class < 15 > 25
Lines per method < 20 > 50
Parameters per method <= 3 > 5
Cyclomatic complexity <= 10 > 15

6. Declaration Standards

Variable Declaration Rules

  • Declare variables at the beginning of their scope (not Java C-style top of method)
  • One declaration per line
  • Initialize when declaring where possible
  • Use var (Java 10+) for obvious types
// One per line
String firstName;
String lastName;

// Not
String firstName, lastName;  // Bad

// Declare at point of use
for (String name : names) {
    String processed = process(name);  // declared where used
    log(processed);
}

// var for obvious types (Java 10+)
var customers = new ArrayList<Customer>();
var orderMap = new HashMap<String, Order>();

Constant Declaration

// Interface constants — avoid (Constant Interface Anti-pattern)
interface Constants {
    int MAX = 100;  // Bad
}

// Class-level constants
public class OrderLimits {
    public static final int MAX_ITEMS_PER_ORDER = 50;
    public static final BigDecimal MAX_ORDER_VALUE = new BigDecimal("10000.00");

    private OrderLimits() {}  // utility class — prevent instantiation
}

// Enum-based constants (preferred when related)
public enum OrderStatus { PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED }

7. Exception Handling Standards

Checked vs Unchecked

  • Use unchecked (RuntimeException) for programming errors and cases callers can't reasonably handle
  • Use checked (Exception) only for conditions the caller must explicitly handle and can recover from
  • Never catch and ignore exceptions silently

Exception Naming

  • Suffix with Exception: OrderNotFoundException, PaymentProcessingException
  • Be specific: avoid generic ServiceException as the only type

Exception Handling Code Standards

// Bad — catch all, swallow
try {
    processOrder();
} catch (Exception e) { }

// Bad — catch all, log only (still swallowed at higher level if unchecked)
try {
    processOrder();
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.error("Error", e);  // but rethrow? or return? unclear
}

// Good — specific catch, meaningful action
try {
    orderService.place(order);
} catch (InsufficientInventoryException e) {
    log.warn("Order {} rejected: insufficient inventory", order.getId());
    return OrderResult.rejected("INSUFFICIENT_INVENTORY");
} catch (PaymentException e) {
    throw new OrderProcessingException("Payment failed for order " + order.getId(), e);
}

8. Concurrency Standards

Thread Safety Documentation

/**
 * Thread-safe. All public methods are synchronized.
 */
public class SafeCounter { ... }

/**
 * NOT thread-safe. External synchronization required.
 */
public class UnsafeCache { ... }

Preferred Concurrency Tools

Need Preferred Tool
Single shared counter AtomicInteger
Thread-safe list CopyOnWriteArrayList
Thread-safe map ConcurrentHashMap
Run tasks ExecutorService (not new Thread())
Coordination CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier
Read-heavy locks ReadWriteLock

9. Resource Management Standards

// Always try-with-resources for AutoCloseable
try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection();
     PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(sql)) {
    // use conn and ps
}  // both auto-closed

// Stream operations that must be closed
try (Stream<String> lines = Files.lines(path)) {
    lines.filter(l -> !l.isBlank()).forEach(System.out::println);
}

10. Standards Enforcement Tools

Tool Purpose Key Config
Checkstyle Formatting/style rules checkstyle.xml
PMD Code quality patterns pmd-ruleset.xml
SpotBugs Bug pattern detection spotbugs-exclude.xml
SonarQube Comprehensive quality gate sonar-project.properties
Google Java Format Auto-formatting Maven/Gradle plugin
EditorConfig IDE-level formatting .editorconfig

Interview Q&A

Q1: What is the difference between Oracle's Java coding conventions and Google's Java style guide? Oracle's conventions are older (1997), use 4-space indentation and 80-char line limit. Google's guide is more modern, uses 2-space indentation, 100-char limit, and is more prescriptive about import ordering and Javadoc. Most modern projects use Google's or a custom variant.

Q2: What is the Constant Interface Anti-pattern? Defining constants inside an interface and then implementing it to inherit the constants. This pollutes implementing classes' namespaces and leaks implementation detail. Use a utility class with private constructor or enums instead.

Q3: Why should you never use wildcard imports? They obscure which types are actually used, can cause name conflicts when two packages have types with the same name, make code harder to navigate, and make refactoring harder. Modern IDEs organize imports automatically.

Q4: What are the rules for exception naming? Suffix with Exception. Name should describe the specific error condition, not just "ServiceException." Examples: CustomerNotFoundException, InsufficientFundsException, OrderValidationException.

Q5: What is cyclomatic complexity and what is an acceptable threshold? A measure of the number of independent paths through code (number of decision points + 1). Threshold: <=10 per method is recommended; >15 indicates a method needs to be broken down. High CC = hard to test and understand.

Q6: Why organize packages by feature rather than by layer? Feature-organized packages group related code (controller, service, repository for one domain) together — making it easy to find all code for a feature. Layer-organized packages spread a feature across the codebase, requiring navigation between packages for any change. Feature packages align better with microservices architecture.

Q7: What is the difference between TODO and FIXME comments? TODO marks intentional missing work to do later (planned enhancement). FIXME marks a known bug or problem that needs immediate or near-term fixing. Both should include a tracking ticket number in professional settings.

Q8: When should you use var in Java 10+? When the type is obvious from the right-hand side (constructor calls, cast expressions, known factory methods). Avoid when the type is not obvious — var result = service.process(data) — the reader can't tell what result is.

Q9: What makes a good method length standard? No universal rule, but methods should fit on one screen (20–30 lines max), do one thing, and have a descriptive name that makes a comment unnecessary. The key metric is: can a reader understand it without scrolling or context-switching?

Q10: How do you handle an exception you can't do anything about? At minimum, log it with context. If it's truly unrecoverable, re-throw it (possibly wrapped). Never silently swallow. If it's a programming error (IllegalArgumentException), fix the code.

Interview Strategy

  1. Name drop the guides: "I follow Google Java Style Guide, enforced with Checkstyle."
  2. Show awareness of tooling: Checkstyle, SonarQube, IDE auto-formatting integration.
  3. Discuss team dynamics: Standards matter most as team conventions — "What standards does your team use?"
  4. Package organization: Feature-by-feature org is a strong answer for modern enterprise Java.
  5. Exception handling is always asked: Know the checked vs. unchecked debate and which you prefer.

Common Pitfalls

  • Wildcard imports in production code
  • catch (Exception e) {} — the silent swallow
  • Constant interface anti-pattern
  • Not documenting thread safety
  • Magic numbers and magic strings throughout code
  • Inconsistent naming (mixing camelCase and snake_case in the same codebase)

Cheat Sheet

Standard Rule
File structure License → package → imports → class
Imports No wildcards; ordered by group
Indentation 4 spaces (Oracle) / 2 spaces (Google)
Line length 100–120 chars max
Naming Classes=Noun UpperCamel, Methods=Verb lowerCamel, Constants=UPPER_SNAKE
JavaDoc All public types and methods
Exceptions Specific types, never swallow, preserve cause
Resources Always try-with-resources
Package org By feature, not by layer
Constants Enum or utility class; never Constant Interface

































Spring Boot 4 & Spring Framework 7 Annotations Interview Guide

Spring Framework

1. Core Spring Framework Annotations

@Component & Stereotype Annotations

@Component              // Generic Spring-managed bean
@Service               // Service layer component
@Repository            // DAO layer component (enables exception translation)
@Controller            // MVC controller component
@RestController         // REST controller (combines @Controller + @ResponseBody)
  • @Component: Base annotation for any Spring-managed bean
  • @Service: Indicates service layer business logic
  • @Repository: Enables DAO exception translation to Spring's DataAccessException
  • @Controller: Traditional MVC controllers returning views
  • @RestController: Returns JSON/XML directly, no view resolution

Dependency Injection Annotations

@Autowired             // Field, constructor, or setter injection
@Qualifier("beanName") // Disambiguate when multiple beans of same type
@Primary               // Mark as primary candidate when multiple beans exist
@Resource(name = "beanName") // JSR-250 injection (by name)
@Inject                // JSR-330 injection (equivalent to @Autowired)
  • @Autowired: Preferred on constructors (required = true by default)
  • @Qualifier: Used with @Autowired to specify bean name
  • @Primary: Marks default bean when multiple candidates exist
  • @Resource: Name-based injection, falls back to type if name not found

Configuration Annotations

@Configuration         // Indicates configuration class
@Bean                  // Declares a bean method
@ComponentScan         // Scans for components (basePackages)
@Import                // Import other configuration classes
@PropertySource        // Load properties file
@Profile               // Bean activation based on active profile

2. Spring Boot 4 Core Annotations

Application Configuration

@SpringBootApplication    // Combines @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan
@EnableAutoConfiguration // Enables Spring Boot's auto-configuration mechanism
@SpringBootConfiguration // Alternative to @Configuration for Boot apps

Properties & Configuration

@ConfigurationProperties // Bind external properties to fields
@Value("${property.key}") // Inject single property value
@EmbeddedId              // Embedded primary key in JPA entities
@Validated               // Enable validation on configuration properties

@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app.datasource")
@Validated
public class DataSourceProperties {

    @NotNull
    private String url;

    @Min(1)
    @Max(100)
    private int maxConnections = 10;

    // Getters and setters
}

Conditional Annotations

@ConditionalOnClass        // Bean exists if class is on classpath
@ConditionalOnMissingClass // Bean exists if class is NOT on classpath
@ConditionalOnBean         // Bean exists if another bean exists
@ConditionalOnMissingBean  // Bean exists if another bean is missing
@ConditionalOnProperty     // Bean exists based on property value
@ConditionalOnResource     // Bean exists if resource is available
@ConditionalOnWebApplication // Bean exists in web context
@ConditionalOnNotWebApplication // Bean exists in non-web context

@Bean
@ConditionalOnProperty(name = "cache.enabled", havingValue = "true")
public CacheManager cacheManager() {
    return new ConcurrentMapCacheManager("users", "products");
}

3. Web & REST Annotations

Request Mapping

@RequestMapping          // Base URL mapping (class/method level)
@GetMapping             // HTTP GET mapping
@PostMapping            // HTTP POST mapping
@PutMapping             // HTTP PUT mapping
@DeleteMapping          // HTTP DELETE mapping
@PatchMapping           // HTTP PATCH mapping

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/v1/users")
public class UserController {

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public User getUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return userService.findById(id);
    }

    @PostMapping
    @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.CREATED)
    public User createUser(@Valid @RequestBody User user) {
        return userService.create(user);
    }
}

Request Parameter Binding

@PathVariable          // Bind URL template variable
@RequestParam          // Bind query parameter
@RequestBody          // Bind HTTP request body
@RequestHeader         // Bind HTTP header
@CookieValue           // Bind HTTP cookie
@MatrixVariable       // Bind matrix variable in URL
@RequestPart          // Bind multipart/form-data part

@GetMapping("/search")
public List<User> searchUsers(
    @RequestParam(required = false) String name,
    @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
    @RequestParam("size") int pageSize,
    @RequestHeader("User-Agent") String userAgent) {
    return userService.search(name, page, pageSize);
}

Response Handling

@ResponseBody         // Return value as response body
@ResponseStatus        // Set HTTP status code
 ResponseEntity<T>    // Full response control (status, headers, body)

@GetMapping("/users/{id}")
public ResponseEntity<User> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
    return userService.findById(id)
        .map(user -> ResponseEntity.ok().header("X-Custom-Header", "value").body(user))
        .orElse(ResponseEntity.notFound().build());
}

Cross-Origin & CORS

@CrossOrigin           // Enable CORS for controller/method
@CrossOrigin(origins = "http://localhost:3000", methods = {GET, POST})

3. Web & REST Annotations

Request Mapping

@RequestMapping          // Base URL mapping
@GetMapping             // HTTP GET mapping
@PostMapping            // HTTP POST mapping
@PutMapping             // HTTP PUT mapping
@DeleteMapping          // HTTP DELETE mapping
@PatchMapping           // HTTP PATCH mapping
@RequestHeader          // Bind HTTP header
@PathVariable          // Path variable binding
@RequestParam           // Request parameter binding
@RequestBody            // Request body binding
@RequestPart            // Multipart request part
@CookieValue            // Cookie value binding

REST Controller Examples

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/users")
public class UserController {

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<User> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
        User user = userService.findById(id);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(user);
    }

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<User> createUser(@Valid @RequestBody UserRequest request) {
        User user = userService.createUser(request);
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(user);
    }

    @PutMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<User> updateUser(@PathVariable Long id, 
                                       @Valid @RequestBody UserRequest request) {
        User user = userService.updateUser(id, request);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(user);
    }

    @DeleteMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<Void> deleteUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
        userService.deleteUser(id);
        return ResponseEntity.noContent().build();
    }

    @GetMapping
    public ResponseEntity<Page<User>> getUsers(
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "10") int size,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "name") String sort) {
        Pageable pageable = PageRequest.of(page, size, Sort.by(sort));
        Page<User> users = userService.findAll(pageable);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(users);
    }
}

Request Parameters & Headers

@RestController
public class RequestController {

    @GetMapping("/search")
    public ResponseEntity<List<User>> searchUsers(
            @RequestParam String query,
            @RequestParam(required = false) String status,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
            @RequestHeader("User-Agent") String userAgent) {

        List<User> users = userService.search(query, status, page);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(users);
    }

    @PostMapping("/upload")
    public ResponseEntity<String> uploadFile(
            @RequestParam("file") MultipartFile file,
            @RequestParam("description") String description) {

        String result = fileService.upload(file, description);
        return ResponseEntity.ok(result);
    }

    @GetMapping("/cookies")
    public ResponseEntity<String> getCookieValue(@CookieValue("session") String sessionId) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Session ID: " + sessionId);
    }
}

Response Handling

@RestController
public class ResponseController {

    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<?> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
        Optional<User> user = userService.findById(id);

        if (user.isPresent()) {
            return ResponseEntity.ok()
                .header("X-Custom-Header", "value")
                .cacheControl(CacheControl.maxAge(300))
                .body(user.get());
        } else {
            return ResponseEntity.notFound().build();
        }
    }

    @GetMapping("/download")
    public ResponseEntity<Resource> downloadFile() {
        Resource file = fileService.getFile();

        return ResponseEntity.ok()
            .header(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_DISPOSITION, "attachment; filename=file.pdf")
            .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_PDF)
            .body(file);
    }

    @GetMapping("/status")
    @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.ACCEPTED)
    public Map<String, Object> getStatus() {
        return Map.of(
            "status", "active",
            "timestamp", System.currentTimeMillis()
        );
    }
}

Cross-Origin & CORS

@RestController
@CrossOrigin(origins = "http://localhost:3000", methods = {GET, POST})
public class CorsController {

    @GetMapping("/public-data")
    public ResponseEntity<String> getPublicData() {
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Public data");
    }

    @PostMapping("/submit-data")
    @CrossOrigin(origins = "http://localhost:3000")
    public ResponseEntity<String> submitData(@RequestBody Data data) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok("Data submitted");
    }
}

@Configuration
public class CorsConfig {

    @Bean
    public CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
        CorsConfiguration configuration = new CorsConfiguration();
        configuration.setAllowedOrigins(Arrays.asList("http://localhost:3000"));
        configuration.setAllowedMethods(Arrays.asList("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"));
        configuration.setAllowedHeaders(Arrays.asList("*"));
        configuration.setAllowCredentials(true);

        UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource source = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
        source.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", configuration);
        return source;
    }
}

5. Conditional Annotations

Spring Boot Conditional Annotations

@ConditionalOnClass        // Bean exists if class is on classpath
@ConditionalOnMissingClass // Bean exists if class is NOT on classpath
@ConditionalOnBean         // Bean exists if another bean exists
@ConditionalOnMissingBean  // Bean exists if another bean is missing
@ConditionalOnProperty     // Bean exists based on property value
@ConditionalOnResource     // Bean exists if resource is available
@ConditionalOnWebApplication // Bean exists in web context
@ConditionalOnNotWebApplication // Bean exists in non-web context
@ConditionalOnJava         // Bean exists based on Java version
@ConditionalOnJndi         // Bean exists if JNDI location exists
@ConditionalOnExpression   // Bean exists based on SpEL expression
@ConditionalOnCloudPlatform // Bean exists on specific cloud platform

Conditional Examples

@Bean
@ConditionalOnProperty(name = "cache.enabled", havingValue = "true")
public CacheManager cacheManager() {
    return new ConcurrentMapCacheManager("users", "products");
}

@Bean
@ConditionalOnClass(name = "redis.clients.jedis.Jedis")
public RedisTemplate<String, Object> redisTemplate() {
    return new RedisTemplate<>();
}

@Bean
@ConditionalOnMissingBean(DataSource.class)
public DataSource defaultDataSource() {
    return new HikariDataSource();
}

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnWebApplication
public class WebConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "cors.enabled", havingValue = "true")
    public CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
        CorsConfiguration configuration = new CorsConfiguration();
        configuration.setAllowedOrigins(Arrays.asList("*"));
        configuration.setAllowedMethods(Arrays.asList("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE"));
        configuration.setAllowedHeaders(Arrays.asList("*"));
        UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource source = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
        source.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", configuration);
        return source;
    }
}

@Configuration
@ConditionalOnNotWebApplication
public class StandaloneConfiguration {

    @Bean
    public CommandLineRunner commandLineRunner() {
        return args -> System.out.println("Running in standalone mode");
    }
}

Custom Conditional Annotations

@Target({ElementType.TYPE, ElementType.METHOD})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@Conditional(OnDatabaseCondition.class)
public @interface ConditionalOnDatabase {
    String value() default "mysql";
}

public class OnDatabaseCondition implements Condition {

    @Override
    public boolean matches(ConditionContext context, AnnotatedTypeMetadata metadata) {
        Map<String, Object> attributes = metadata.getAnnotationAttributes(
            ConditionalOnDatabase.class.getName());
        String database = (String) attributes.get("value");

        String property = context.getEnvironment().getProperty("app.database.type");
        return database.equals(property);
    }
}

@Configuration
public class DatabaseConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnDatabase("mysql")
    public DataSource mysqlDataSource() {
        return new HikariDataSource();
    }

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnDatabase("postgresql")
    public DataSource postgresqlDataSource() {
        return new HikariDataSource();
    }
}

6. Data Access Annotations

JPA Entity Mapping

@Entity                    // JPA entity class
@Table(name = "users")     // Specify table name
@Id                        // Primary key
@GeneratedValue            // Auto-generate primary key
@Column                    // Column specification
@Transient                 // Non-persistent field
@Embedded                  // Embedded object
@Enumerated                // Enum mapping
@Lob                       // Large object (BLOB/CLOB)
@Temporal                  // Date/time mapping
@Basic                     // Basic property mapping
@Access                    // Property vs field access
@Version                   // Optimistic locking version

Comprehensive Entity Example

@Entity
@Table(name = "employees", 
       indexes = {
           @Index(name = "idx_employee_email", columnList = "email"),
           @Index(name = "idx_employee_department", columnList = "department")
       },
       uniqueConstraints = {
           @UniqueConstraint(columnNames = "email")
       })
public class Employee {

    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    @Column(name = "first_name", nullable = false, length = 50)
    private String firstName;

    @Column(name = "last_name", nullable = false, length = 50)
    private String lastName;

    @Column(name = "email", nullable = false, unique = true, length = 100)
    private String email;

    @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)
    @Column(name = "department", nullable = false)
    private Department department;

    @Column(name = "salary", precision = 10, scale = 2)
    private BigDecimal salary;

    @Lob
    @Column(name = "profile_picture")
    private byte[] profilePicture;

    @Temporal(TemporalType.TIMESTAMP)
    @Column(name = "hire_date", nullable = false)
    private Date hireDate;

    @Temporal(TemporalType.TIMESTAMP)
    @Column(name = "last_login")
    private Date lastLogin;

    @Version
    private Long version;

    @Transient
    private String fullName; // Not persisted

    @Embedded
    private Address address;

    // Getters and setters
}

@Embeddable
public class Address {
    @Column(name = "street", length = 200)
    private String street;

    @Column(name = "city", length = 100)
    private String city;

    @Column(name = "state", length = 50)
    private String state;

    @Column(name = "zip_code", length = 10)
    private String zipCode;
}

Relationship Mapping

@Entity
public class Department {

    @Id
    @GeneratedValue
    private Long id;

    @Column(nullable = false, unique = true)
    private String name;

    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "department", cascade = CascadeType.ALL, fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    private List<Employee> employees = new ArrayList<>();

    @OneToOne(mappedBy = "department")
    private DepartmentHead head;
}

@Entity
public class Employee {

    @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    @JoinColumn(name = "department_id", nullable = false)
    private Department department;

    @OneToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY, cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    @JoinColumn(name = "employee_details_id")
    private EmployeeDetails details;

    @ManyToMany(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    @JoinTable(
        name = "employee_projects",
        joinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "employee_id"),
        inverseJoinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "project_id")
    )
    private Set<Project> projects = new HashSet<>();
}

Spring Data JPA Repository Annotations

@Repository                  // Spring Data repository
@EnableJpaRepositories        // Enable JPA repositories
@Transactional               // Transaction management
@Query                      // Custom JPQL query
@Modifying                  // Query modifies data
@Param                      // Named parameter in query
@Lock                       // Pessimistic locking
@QueryHints                 // JPA query hints
@Procedure                  // Stored procedure call
@QueryRewriter              // Custom query rewriting

Repository Interface Examples

@Repository
public interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long>, JpaSpecificationExecutor<User> {

    // Derived query methods
    Optional<User> findByEmail(String email);
    List<User> findByFirstNameAndLastName(String firstName, String lastName);
    List<User> findByDepartmentAndSalaryGreaterThan(Department department, BigDecimal salary);

    // Custom JPQL queries
    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email AND u.active = true")
    Optional<User> findByEmailAndActive(@Param("email") String email);

    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.department = :dept ORDER BY u.salary DESC")
    List<User> findByDepartmentOrderBySalary(@Param("dept") Department department);

    // Native SQL queries
    @Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users u WHERE u.email LIKE %?1%", nativeQuery = true)
    List<User> findByEmailContaining(String emailPattern);

    // Modifying queries
    @Modifying
    @Transactional
    @Query("UPDATE User u SET u.lastLogin = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP WHERE u.id = :id")
    int updateLastLogin(@Param("id") Long userId);

    @Modifying
    @Transactional
    @Query("DELETE FROM User u WHERE u.active = false AND u.lastLogin < :cutoffDate")
    int deleteInactiveUsers(@Param("cutoffDate") LocalDateTime cutoffDate);

    // Count queries
    @Query("SELECT COUNT(u) FROM User u WHERE u.department = :dept")
    long countByDepartment(@Param("dept") Department department);

    // Pagination
    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.department = :dept")
    Page<User> findByDepartment(@Param("dept") Department department, Pageable pageable);

    // Locking
    @Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.id = :id")
    Optional<User> findByIdForUpdate(@Param("id") Long id);

    // Query hints
    @QueryHints(@QueryHint(name = org.hibernate.jpa.QueryHints.HINT_CACHEABLE, value = "true"))
    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.department = :dept")
    List<User> findByDepartmentWithCache(@Param("dept") Department department);

    // Stored procedure
    @Procedure(name = "calculate_user_bonus")
    BigDecimal calculateUserBonus(@Param("user_id") Long userId, @Param("year") int year);
}

Custom Repository Implementation

public interface UserRepositoryCustom {
    List<User> findUsersWithComplexCriteria(String searchTerm, Department department, BigDecimal minSalary);
    void bulkUpdateDepartment(Long oldDeptId, Long newDeptId);
}

@Repository
public class UserRepositoryImpl implements UserRepositoryCustom {

    @PersistenceContext
    private EntityManager entityManager;

    @Override
    public List<User> findUsersWithComplexCriteria(String searchTerm, Department department, BigDecimal minSalary) {
        CriteriaBuilder cb = entityManager.getCriteriaBuilder();
        CriteriaQuery<User> query = cb.createQuery(User.class);
        Root<User> root = query.from(User.class);

        List<Predicate> predicates = new ArrayList<>();

        if (searchTerm != null) {
            predicates.add(cb.or(
                cb.like(cb.lower(root.get("firstName")), "%" + searchTerm.toLowerCase() + "%"),
                cb.like(cb.lower(root.get("lastName")), "%" + searchTerm.toLowerCase() + "%"),
                cb.like(cb.lower(root.get("email")), "%" + searchTerm.toLowerCase() + "%")
            ));
        }

        if (department != null) {
            predicates.add(cb.equal(root.get("department"), department));
        }

        if (minSalary != null) {
            predicates.add(cb.greaterThanOrEqualTo(root.get("salary"), minSalary));
        }

        query.where(predicates.toArray(new Predicate[0]));
        return entityManager.createQuery(query).getResultList();
    }

    @Override
    @Transactional
    public void bulkUpdateDepartment(Long oldDeptId, Long newDeptId) {
        String jpql = "UPDATE User u SET u.department.id = :newDeptId WHERE u.department.id = :oldDeptId";
        entityManager.createQuery(jpql)
            .setParameter("newDeptId", newDeptId)
            .setParameter("oldDeptId", oldDeptId)
            .executeUpdate();
    }
}

Transaction Management Annotations

@Transactional               // Method/class-level transaction
@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED)
@Transactional(timeout = 30)
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)
@Transactional(noRollbackFor = BusinessException.class)
@Transactional(value = "transactionManager")

Transaction Examples

@Service
public class PaymentService {

    @Transactional(
        propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED,
        isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED,
        timeout = 30,
        rollbackFor = {PaymentException.class, InsufficientFundsException.class}
    )
    public Payment processPayment(PaymentRequest request) {
        // Payment processing logic
        Account fromAccount = accountRepository.findById(request.getFromAccountId())
            .orElseThrow(() -> new AccountNotFoundException(request.getFromAccountId()));

        Account toAccount = accountRepository.findById(request.getToAccountId())
            .orElseThrow(() -> new AccountNotFoundException(request.getToAccountId()));

        // Validate sufficient funds
        if (fromAccount.getBalance().compareTo(request.getAmount()) < 0) {
            throw new InsufficientFundsException("Insufficient funds");
        }

        // Perform transfer
        fromAccount.setBalance(fromAccount.getBalance().subtract(request.getAmount()));
        toAccount.setBalance(toAccount.getBalance().add(request.getAmount()));

        accountRepository.save(fromAccount);
        accountRepository.save(toAccount);

        return createPaymentRecord(request);
    }

    @Transactional(readOnly = true)
    public List<Payment> getPaymentHistory(Long userId) {
        return paymentRepository.findByUserId(userId);
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void auditPayment(Payment payment) {
        // Always runs in a new transaction
        auditRepository.logPayment(payment);
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NOT_SUPPORTED)
    public List<Payment> generatePaymentReport(LocalDate startDate, LocalDate endDate) {
        // Runs without transaction (for large data reads)
        return paymentRepository.findByDateRange(startDate, endDate);
    }
}

JDBC Template Annotations

@Repository
public class JdbcUserRepository {

    private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;
    private final SimpleJdbcInsert userInsert;
    private final SimpleJdbcCall getUserProcedure;

    public JdbcUserRepository(JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate) {
        this.jdbcTemplate = jdbcTemplate;
        this.userInsert = new SimpleJdbcInsert(jdbcTemplate)
            .withTableName("users")
            .usingGeneratedKeyColumns("id");

        this.getUserProcedure = new SimpleJdbcCall(jdbcTemplate)
            .withProcedureName("get_user_details")
            .declareParameters(
                new SqlParameter("p_user_id", Types.BIGINT),
                new SqlOutParameter("p_user_data", Types.REF_CURSOR, new UserRowMapper())
            );
    }

    public User save(User user) {
        Map<String, Object> parameters = new HashMap<>();
        parameters.put("first_name", user.getFirstName());
        parameters.put("last_name", user.getLastName());
        parameters.put("email", user.getEmail());
        parameters.put("department_id", user.getDepartment().getId());

        Number id = userInsert.executeAndReturnKey(parameters);
        user.setId(id.longValue());
        return user;
    }

    public Optional<User> findById(Long id) {
        String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?";
        try {
            User user = jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(sql, new UserRowMapper(), id);
            return Optional.of(user);
        } catch (EmptyResultDataAccessException e) {
            return Optional.empty();
        }
    }

    public List<User> findByDepartment(Long departmentId) {
        String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE department_id = ?";
        return jdbcTemplate.query(sql, new UserRowMapper(), departmentId);
    }

    @Transactional
    public int update(User user) {
        String sql = "UPDATE users SET first_name = ?, last_name = ?, email = ?, department_id = ? WHERE id = ?";
        return jdbcTemplate.update(sql, 
            user.getFirstName(), 
            user.getLastName(), 
            user.getEmail(), 
            user.getDepartment().getId(), 
            user.getId());
    }

    @Transactional
    public int deleteById(Long id) {
        String sql = "DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ?";
        return jdbcTemplate.update(sql, id);
    }

    public User callGetUserProcedure(Long userId) {
        SqlParameterSource in = new MapSqlParameterSource()
            .addValue("p_user_id", userId);

        Map<String, Object> out = getUserProcedure.execute(in);
        return (User) out.get("p_user_data");
    }

    private static class UserRowMapper implements RowMapper<User> {
        @Override
        public User mapRow(ResultSet rs, int rowNum) throws SQLException {
            User user = new User();
            user.setId(rs.getLong("id"));
            user.setFirstName(rs.getString("first_name"));
            user.setLastName(rs.getString("last_name"));
            user.setEmail(rs.getString("email"));
            // Map other fields
            return user;
        }
    }
}

Database Initialization

@Profile("dev")
@Configuration
public class DatabaseInitializer {

    @Bean
    public DataSourceInitializer dataSourceInitializer(DataSource dataSource) {
        DataSourceInitializer initializer = new DataSourceInitializer();
        initializer.setDataSource(dataSource);
        initializer.setDatabasePopulator(databasePopulator());
        return initializer;
    }

    private DatabasePopulator databasePopulator() {
        ResourceDatabasePopulator populator = new ResourceDatabasePopulator();
        populator.addScript(new ClassPathResource("schema.sql"));
        populator.addScript(new ClassPathResource("data.sql"));
        populator.setSeparator(";");
        return populator;
    }
}

// Using Flyway for database migrations
@Configuration
public class FlywayConfig {

    @Bean
    @ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "spring.flyway")
    public Flyway flyway(DataSource dataSource) {
        return Flyway.configure()
            .dataSource(dataSource)
            .baselineOnMigrate(true)
            .validateOnMigrate(true)
            .load();
    }

    @Bean
    public FlywayMigrationStrategy flywayMigrationStrategy() {
        return flyway -> {
            // Custom migration logic
            flyway.migrate();
        };
    }
}

JPA Event Listeners

@EntityListeners(AuditListener.class)
@Entity
public class AuditableEntity {

    @CreatedDate
    @Column(name = "created_at", updatable = false)
    private LocalDateTime createdAt;

    @LastModifiedDate
    @Column(name = "updated_at")
    private LocalDateTime updatedAt;

    @CreatedBy
    @Column(name = "created_by", updatable = false)
    private String createdBy;

    @LastModifiedBy
    @Column(name = "updated_by")
    private String updatedBy;
}

@Component
public class AuditListener {

    @PrePersist
    public void prePersist(Object entity) {
        if (entity instanceof AuditableEntity) {
            AuditableEntity auditable = (AuditableEntity) entity;
            auditable.setCreatedAt(LocalDateTime.now());
            auditable.setCreatedBy(getCurrentUser());
        }
    }

    @PreUpdate
    public void preUpdate(Object entity) {
        if (entity instanceof AuditableEntity) {
            AuditableEntity auditable = (AuditableEntity) entity;
            auditable.setUpdatedAt(LocalDateTime.now());
            auditable.setUpdatedBy(getCurrentUser());
        }
    }

    private String getCurrentUser() {
        return SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().getName();
    }
}

Data Access Best Practices

  • Use appropriate fetch types - LAZY for associations, EAGER for essential data
  • Implement proper transaction boundaries - Keep transactions short and focused
  • Use connection pooling - Configure HikariCP for optimal performance
  • Batch operations for bulk updates - Use @BatchSize and JDBC batch updates
  • Optimize queries - Use proper indexing and avoid N+1 problems
  • Handle exceptions properly - Use Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy
  • Use DTOs for API responses - Avoid exposing entities directly
  • Implement proper caching - Use @Cacheable for frequently accessed data

4. Validation Annotations

Bean Validation (JSR-380)

@Valid                 // Validate nested object
@NotNull              // Field must not be null
@NotEmpty             // String/collection must not be empty
@NotBlank             // String must not be blank
@Size(min=, max=)     // Size constraints
@Min(value) / @Max    // Numeric bounds
@Email                // Valid email format
@Pattern(regexp)      // Regular expression
@Future / @Past       // Date constraints
@Positive / @Negative // Positive/negative numbers
@AssertTrue           // Must be true

public class UserRegistrationRequest {

    @NotBlank(message = "Username is required")
    @Size(min = 3, max = 20, message = "Username must be between 3 and 20 characters")
    private String username;

    @Email(message = "Invalid email format")
    @NotBlank(message = "Email is required")
    private String email;

    @NotNull(message = "Age is required")
    @Min(value = 18, message = "Must be at least 18 years old")
    private Integer age;

    @Valid
    @NotNull(message = "Address is required")
    private Address address;
}

Custom Validation

@Target({ElementType.FIELD, ElementType.PARAMETER})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@Constraint(validatedBy = UniqueUsernameValidator.class)
public @interface UniqueUsername {
    String message() default "Username must be unique";
    Class<?>[] groups() default {};
    Class<? extends Payload>[] payload() default {};
}

9. Testing Annotations

Spring Boot Testing Annotations

@SpringBootTest         // Full Spring Boot test context
@WebMvcTest            // Web layer test (controllers only)
@DataJpaTest           // JPA repository test
@JdbcTest              // JDBC repository test
@DataMongoTest         // MongoDB test
@RestClientTest        // REST client test
@JsonTest              // JSON serialization test
@MockBean              // Mock a bean in test context
@MockitoBean           // Mockito mock bean (Spring Boot 3+)
@SpyBean               // Spy on a bean in test context
@TestConfiguration    // Test-specific configuration
@TestPropertySource     // Test-specific properties
@AutoConfigureTestDatabase // Auto-configure test database
@AutoConfigureMockMvc   // Auto-configure MockMvc
@AutoConfigureWebTestClient // Auto-configure WebTestClient

JUnit 5 Testing Annotations

@Test                  // Test method
@DisplayName            // Test display name
@ParameterizedTest      // Parameterized test
@RepeatedTest           // Repeated test execution
@Disabled               // Disabled test
@Tag                    // Test tag for filtering
@ExtendWith             // Register extension
@TempDir               // Temporary directory
@TestInstance         // Test instance lifecycle
@BeforeAll             // Before all tests
@AfterAll              // After all tests
@BeforeEach            // Before each test
@AfterEach             // After each test

Testing Examples

@SpringBootTest
@AutoConfigureMockMvc
@TestPropertySource(properties = {
    "spring.datasource.url=jdbc:h2:mem:testdb",
    "spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=create-drop"
})
public class UserControllerTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should create user successfully")
    void shouldCreateUser() throws Exception {
        // Given
        UserRequest request = new UserRequest("testuser", "test@example.com");
        User createdUser = new User(1L, "testuser", "test@example.com");

        when(userService.createUser(any(UserRequest.class))).thenReturn(createdUser);

        // When & Then
        mockMvc.perform(post("/api/users")
                .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
                .content(objectMapper.writeValueAsString(request)))
                .andExpect(status().isCreated())
                .andExpect(jsonPath("$.id", is(1)))
                .andExpect(jsonPath("$.username", is("testuser")));

        verify(userService).createUser(any(UserRequest.class));
    }

    @ParameterizedTest
    @ValueSource(strings = {"user1", "user2", "user3"})
    @DisplayName("Should find user by username")
    void shouldFindUserByUsername(String username) {
        // Given
        User user = new User(1L, username, username + "@example.com");
        when(userService.findByUsername(username)).thenReturn(Optional.of(user));

        // When
        Optional<User> result = userService.findByUsername(username);

        // Then
        assertTrue(result.isPresent());
        assertEquals(username, result.get().getUsername());
    }
}

@DataJpaTest
@AutoConfigureTestDatabase(replace = AutoConfigureTestDatabase.Replace.NONE)
public class UserRepositoryTest {

    @Autowired
    private TestEntityManager entityManager;

    @Autowired
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should find user by email")
    void shouldFindByEmail() {
        // Given
        User user = new User("testuser", "test@example.com");
        entityManager.persistAndFlush(user);

        // When
        Optional<User> result = userRepository.findByEmail("test@example.com");

        // Then
        assertTrue(result.isPresent());
        assertEquals("test@example.com", result.get().getEmail());
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should count users by status")
    void shouldCountByStatus() {
        // Given
        User user1 = new User("user1", "user1@example.com");
        user1.setStatus(UserStatus.ACTIVE);

        User user2 = new User("user2", "user2@example.com");
        user2.setStatus(UserStatus.ACTIVE);

        User user3 = new User("user3", "user3@example.com");
        user3.setStatus(UserStatus.INACTIVE);

        entityManager.persist(user1);
        entityManager.persist(user2);
        entityManager.persist(user3);
        entityManager.flush();

        // When
        Long activeCount = userRepository.countByStatus(UserStatus.ACTIVE);

        // Then
        assertEquals(2L, activeCount);
    }
}

Integration Testing

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
@TestPropertySource(properties = {
    "spring.cloud.config.enabled=false",
    "spring.cloud.discovery.enabled=false"
})
public class UserIntegrationTest {

    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;

    @Autowired
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should create and retrieve user via REST API")
    void shouldCreateAndRetrieveUser() {
        // Given
        UserRequest request = new UserRequest("integrationuser", "integration@example.com");

        // When - Create user
        ResponseEntity<User> createResponse = restTemplate.postForEntity(
            "/api/users", request, User.class);

        // Then - Verify creation
        assertEquals(HttpStatus.CREATED, createResponse.getStatusCode());
        User createdUser = createResponse.getBody();
        assertNotNull(createdUser);
        assertEquals("integrationuser", createdUser.getUsername());

        // When - Retrieve user
        ResponseEntity<User> getResponse = restTemplate.getForEntity(
            "/api/users/" + createdUser.getId(), User.class);

        // Then - Verify retrieval
        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, getResponse.getStatusCode());
        User retrievedUser = getResponse.getBody();
        assertNotNull(retrievedUser);
        assertEquals(createdUser.getId(), retrievedUser.getId());
    }
}

---

## 10. Caching Annotations

### Cache Abstraction
```java
@EnableCaching          // Enable caching support
@Cacheable             // Cache method result
@CachePut              // Update cache with method result
@CacheEvict            // Remove entry from cache
@Caching               // Multiple cache operations
@CacheConfig           // Class-level cache configuration

Caching Examples

@Service
@CacheConfig(cacheNames = "users")
public class UserService {

    @Cacheable(key = "#id")
    public User findById(Long id) {
        // Database lookup
        return userRepository.findById(id);
    }

    @CachePut(key = "#result.id")
    public User updateUser(User user) {
        // Update in database
        return userRepository.save(user);
    }

    @CacheEvict(key = "#id")
    public void deleteUser(Long id) {
        userRepository.deleteById(id);
    }

    @CacheEvict(allEntries = true)
    public void clearCache() {
        // Clear all cache entries
    }

    @Caching(
        cacheable = @Cacheable(key = "#username"),
        evict = @CacheEvict(key = "#result.id")
    )
    public User createOrUpdateUser(String username, User user) {
        // Complex caching logic
        return userRepository.save(user);
    }
}

@Configuration
@EnableCaching
public class CacheConfig {

    @Bean
    public CacheManager cacheManager() {
        SimpleCacheManager cacheManager = new SimpleCacheManager();
        cacheManager.setCaches(Arrays.asList(
            new ConcurrentMapCache("users"),
            new ConcurrentMapCache("products"),
            new ConcurrentMapCache("orders")
        ));
        return cacheManager;
    }

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnProperty(name = "cache.type", havingValue = "redis")
    public RedisCacheManager redisCacheManager(RedisConnectionFactory connectionFactory) {
        return RedisCacheManager.builder(connectionFactory)
            .cacheDefaults(RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
                .entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(10)))
            .build();
    }
}

---

## 11. Scheduling & Asynchronous Annotations

### Scheduling
```java
@EnableScheduling        // Enable scheduled tasks
@EnableAsync             // Enable async support
@Scheduled              // Schedule method execution
@Async                  // Async method execution
@Timed                  // Timed execution
@EventListener          // Event listener
@AsyncEventListener      // Async event listener

Scheduling Examples

@Configuration
@EnableScheduling
@EnableAsync
public class SchedulingConfig {

    @Bean
    public TaskExecutor taskExecutor() {
        ThreadPoolTaskExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
        executor.setCorePoolSize(5);
        executor.setMaxPoolSize(10);
        executor.setQueueCapacity(25);
        executor.setThreadNamePrefix("async-");
        executor.initialize();
        return executor;
    }

    @Bean
    public ScheduledExecutorService scheduledExecutorService() {
        return Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(5);
    }
}

@Service
public class ScheduledTasks {

    private final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(ScheduledTasks.class);

    @Scheduled(fixedRate = 5000) // Execute every 5 seconds
    public void reportCurrentTime() {
        log.info("The time is now {}", LocalDateTime.now());
    }

    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 10000) // Execute 10 seconds after previous completion
    public void processBatch() {
        log.info("Processing batch job");
        // Batch processing logic
    }

    @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 12 * * MON-FRI") // Weekdays at noon
    public void weekdayNoonTask() {
        log.info("Running weekday noon task");
        // Business logic
    }

    @Scheduled(cron = "${app.backup.cron:0 0 2 * * *}") // Configurable cron
    public void backupDatabase() {
        log.info("Starting database backup");
        // Backup logic
    }

    @Scheduled(initialDelay = 1000, fixedRate = 5000)
    public void delayedAndRepeatedTask() {
        log.info("Delayed and repeated task");
    }
}

@Service
public class AsyncService {

    private final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(AsyncService.class);

    @Async("taskExecutor")
    public CompletableFuture<String> processAsync(String input) {
        log.info("Processing {} asynchronously", input);

        try {
            Thread.sleep(2000); // Simulate long-running task
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
        }

        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture("Processed: " + input);
    }

    @Async
    public void sendEmailAsync(String to, String subject, String body) {
        log.info("Sending email to {}", to);
        // Email sending logic
    }

    @Async
    @Retryable(value = {Exception.class}, maxAttempts = 3, backoff = @Backoff(delay = 1000))
    public void unreliableOperation() {
        // Operation that might fail
        if (Math.random() > 0.7) {
            throw new RuntimeException("Random failure");
        }
        log.info("Operation succeeded");
    }
}

@Component
public class EventListeners {

    @EventListener
    @Async
    public void handleUserCreated(UserCreatedEvent event) {
        log.info("Handling user created event asynchronously: {}", event.getUserId());
        // Send welcome email, update statistics, etc.
    }

    @EventListener(condition = "#event.priority == 'HIGH'")
    public void handleHighPriorityEvent(PriorityEvent event) {
        log.info("Handling high priority event: {}", event.getMessage());
        // Handle high priority events immediately
    }

    @EventListener
    @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT)
    public void handleAfterCommit(DomainEvent event) {
